series.title: Docs/mm/damon: document exclusivity of special-purpose modules
series.author: Liew Rui Yan <aethernet65535@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm/damon: document exclusivity of special-purpose modules

review.summary: Documentation-only patch that adds a section to design.rst documenting the exclusive-start behavior of DAMON special-purpose modules, adds cross-references from lru_sort.rst, reclaim.rst, and stat.rst, and fixes a Sphinx :depth: syntax error.
review.review_inline:
Author: Liew Rui Yan <aethernet65535@gmail.com>

Docs/mm/damon: document exclusivity of special-purpose modules

The Sphinx syntax fix and the cross-reference plumbing look correct.
One accuracy concern follows.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/damon/design.rst b/Documentation/mm/damon/design.rst

[ ... ]

> +.. _damon_design_special_purpose_modules_exclusivity:
> +
> +Note that these modules currently run in an exclusive manner.  If one of those
> +is already running, others will return ``-EBUSY`` upon start requests.

The phrase "if one of those is already running" implies mutual exclusivity
only among the three special-purpose modules (lru_sort, reclaim, stat).
But the actual check in damon_start() is:

    if ((exclusive && nr_running_ctxs) || ...)
        return -EBUSY;

nr_running_ctxs counts *all* active DAMON contexts, including any
user-space DAMON session started via the sysfs interface.  So a user
running their own DAMON monitoring through sysfs will also prevent any
of these modules from starting, and likewise an exclusive module already
running will block subsequent non-exclusive sysfs starts
(running_exclusive_ctxs path).

The same imprecision appears in the three admin-guide files:

> +Note that this module (damon_lru_sort) cannot run simultaneously with other
> +DAMON-based special-purpose modules.

This understates the actual restriction.  A user might read this as
"safe to enable alongside my own DAMON sysfs session", which is wrong.

Suggested wording for design.rst:

    Note that these modules request exclusive DAMON access on start.
    If any DAMON context is already running (whether started by another
    special-purpose module or via the sysfs interface), the start
    request will return ``-EBUSY``.

And for the three admin-guide files, something like:

    Note that this module cannot run simultaneously with any other active
    DAMON context, including other special-purpose modules and user-space
    DAMON sessions started via the sysfs interface.

Also a minor nit on the commit message: it says the cross-references are
added "at the end of their respective Example sections", but damon_stat
has no Examples section -- the note is placed inside the parameter
description block.  The placement looks reasonable; only the commit
message description is slightly off.

series.title: Docs/mm: document Shared Memory Filesystem
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Shared Memory Filesystem

review.summary: This patch fills in the Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst stub with an overview of the shared memory filesystem covering page cache integration, swap, huge page support, accounting, and fallocate. The technical content is mostly accurate but contains a few imprecise statements worth fixing.
review.review_inline:
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Shared Memory Filesystem

Fill in the shmfs.rst stub with an overview of the shared memory
filesystem (tmpfs/shmem).

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst b/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst
> --- a/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst

[ ... ]

> +This design means tmpfs files consume no disk space — their size is bounded
> +only by available memory and swap.  It also means tmpfs data does not

This isn't quite right for mounts with a size= limit.  When max_blocks
is set, shmem_inode_acct_blocks() enforces it and allocations fail with
EINOSPC well before memory is exhausted.  The next section covers this,
but the sentence here contradicts it.  Something like "by available
memory, swap, and any configured mount limit" would be more accurate.

> +Each tmpfs inode tracks two key counters: allocated pages (resident in
> +memory) and swapped pages (evicted to swap).  These are maintained by
> +the ``shmem_charge()`` and ``shmem_uncharge()`` accounting functions,
> +which keep the inode's block usage consistent with the filesystem's mount
> +limits.

shmem_charge() calls shmem_recalc_inode(inode, pages, 0), updating only
the alloced counter and the used_blocks limit.  shmem_uncharge() calls
shmem_recalc_inode(inode, 0, 0), which updates neither counter directly.
The swapped counter in shmem_inode_info is incremented by callers of
shmem_recalc_inode() that pass a non-zero swapped argument (e.g.
shmem_writeout paths), not by shmem_charge/shmem_uncharge.  Attributing
both counters to these two functions is misleading.  Could this be
rephrased to describe what shmem_charge() and shmem_uncharge() actually
do (enforce block limits and trigger freed-page recalculation), and
separately note that shmem_recalc_inode() reconciles alloced vs. swapped
to compute how many pages have been freed?

[ ... ]

> +- ``never``: only base pages (default).

The per-mount default is TMPFS_HUGE_DEFAULT, which is determined at
build time by CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE_SHMEM_HUGE_*.  It is "never"
in the common case (and when THP is not configured), but it can be
"always", "within_size", or "advise" depending on the kernel
configuration.  Could you qualify this, e.g. "never: only base pages
(the default for most configurations)"?

[ ... ]

> +tmpfs supports ``fallocate()`` to preallocate space for a file.
> +Preallocated pages are allocated and inserted into the page cache
> +immediately, guaranteeing that subsequent writes will not fail with
> +``ENOSPC``.

shmem_fallocate() also accepts FALLOC_FL_KEEP_SIZE (without PUNCH_HOLE),
which preallocates pages beyond i_size without extending it.  Worth
mentioning for completeness alongside the basic preallocation use case.

series.title: mm/damon: guard THP-specific DAMOS actions with CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
series.author: <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v1] mm/damon: guard THP-specific DAMOS actions with CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE

review.summary: Guards DAMOS_HUGEPAGE and DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE with CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE since hugepage_madvise() stubs to -EINVAL without that config, making those DAMOS actions silently broken. The enum members, sysfs name table, and switch cases in vaddr.c are all consistently guarded.
review.review_inline:
From: Asier Gutierrez <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>

mm/damon: guard THP-specific DAMOS actions with CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE

The motivation is sound: hugepage_madvise() has a stub that returns
-EINVAL when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is not set, so offering
DAMOS_HUGEPAGE and DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE to users on such kernels is
misleading at best.  All three affected call sites -- the enum in
damon.h, the action name table in sysfs-schemes.c, and the switch
in vaddr.c -- are consistently guarded, and the sysfs interface
matches actions by string name rather than by integer ordinal, so
the resulting shift in enum values for DAMOS_LRU_PRIO and later
entries does not affect any ABI.

One thing was missed:

> diff --git a/include/linux/damon.h b/include/linux/damon.h
> --- a/include/linux/damon.h
> +++ b/include/linux/damon.h
> @@ -138,8 +138,10 @@ enum damos_action {
>  	DAMOS_WILLNEED,
>  	DAMOS_COLD,
>  	DAMOS_PAGEOUT,
> +#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
>  	DAMOS_HUGEPAGE,
>  	DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE,
> +#endif

The kernel-doc comment block above the enum still lists @DAMOS_HUGEPAGE
and @DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE unconditionally:

 * @DAMOS_HUGEPAGE:    Call ``madvise()`` for the region with MADV_HUGEPAGE.
 * @DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE:  Call ``madvise()`` for the region with MADV_NOHUGEPAGE.

When CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is not set those members do not
exist in the enum, so kernel-doc may warn about the dangling
documentation.  Could those two lines be wrapped in a
/* ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE */ note, or at least augmented
with a "(only when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is enabled)" clause
similar to what the design.rst update does?

series.title: Docs/mm: document Swap
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Swap

review.summary: This patch fills in the Documentation/mm/swap.rst stub with a high-level description of the swap subsystem covering swap entries, devices, cluster allocation, the swap cache, zswap, zero-filled pages, and swap I/O. The description is broadly correct but contains a wrong cluster size, a broken cross-reference, and a misleading characterisation of the swap table entries.
review.review_inline:
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Swap

Thanks for filling in this stub.  A few things need correction before
this is accurate.

> +Cluster Allocation
> +------------------
> +
> +Swap space is allocated in clusters (groups of contiguous slots, typically
> +32 pages).

This is wrong.  SWAPFILE_CLUSTER is defined in mm/swap.h as:

    #ifdef CONFIG_THP_SWAP
    #define SWAPFILE_CLUSTER    HPAGE_PMD_NR
    #else
    #define SWAPFILE_CLUSTER    256
    #endif

With CONFIG_THP_SWAP (the common case on x86_64) each cluster is 512
slots; without it, 256.  The value 32 does not appear anywhere in the
swap cluster code.

> +The swap cache is implemented as a per-cluster array of pointers
> +(the "swap table"), providing O(1) lookup by swap entry.
> +See also Documentation/mm/swap-table.rst.

Two issues here.

First, the swap table entries (atomic_long_t in struct swap_table) are
not plain pointers.  Each entry encodes one of four states via type
bits: NULL (free slot), Shadow (swapped-out, XA-compatible value plus
refcount), PFN (cached folio PFN plus refcount), or Bad.  The refcount
for copy-on-write sharing is packed into the same word.  Describing
them as "pointers" loses the most interesting part of the design.

Second, Documentation/mm/swap-table.rst does not exist.  The
implementation lives in mm/swap_table.h (an internal header).  Either
drop the cross-reference or create that document first.

series.title: Docs/mm: document Page Reclaim
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Page Reclaim

review.summary: This patch fills in the page_reclaim.rst documentation stub with a broad overview of the reclaim subsystem covering kswapd, direct reclaim, LRU lists, shrinkers, and working set detection. The content is largely accurate but contains one factual error regarding the data structure used by the working set shadow entry shrinker.
review.review_inline:
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Page Reclaim

Filling in the page_reclaim.rst stub.  The content is generally accurate
and a good read.  One factual error and one imprecision below.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst b/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst

[ ... ]

> +Shadow entries consume a small amount of memory.  To prevent them from
> +accumulating indefinitely, a shrinker reclaims shadow entries from page
> +cache radix tree nodes that contain only shadows and no actual pages.

The kernel replaced the radix tree with xarray several kernel releases
ago.  workingset.c tracks these via struct xa_node, xa_lock, and
XA_CHUNK_SHIFT throughout -- there is no radix_tree_node in sight.
Please s/radix tree nodes/xarray nodes/ (or simply "page cache tree
nodes" if you want to stay implementation-agnostic).

[ ... ]

> +Classic LRU
> +-----------
> +
> +The classic scheme uses four LRU lists per lruvec: active and inactive for
> +both anonymous and file-backed pages.  This approximates a second-chance
> +(clock) algorithm:
> +
> +- Pages start on the inactive list when first allocated.

A nit: pages are placed on the inactive LRU when they are first added
to the LRU (i.e. at fault time for anonymous pages, or at page-cache
add time for file pages), not at allocation time.  Allocation and LRU
insertion are distinct events -- a freshly allocated page has no LRU
membership at all until folio_add_lru() is called.  Something like
"Pages start on the inactive list when first added to the LRU" would
be more precise.

series.title: Docs/mm: document Virtually Contiguous Memory Allocation
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Virtually Contiguous Memory Allocation

review.summary: This patch fills in the vmalloc.rst stub with an overview of vmalloc internals, covering virtual address management, guard pages, huge page support, lazy TLB flushing, per-CPU allocations, vmap, freeing, page table management, NUMA awareness, and KASAN integration. Several technical claims do not match the current implementation.
review.review_inline:
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Virtually Contiguous Memory Allocation

Thanks for filling in this stub.  A few technical points need
correction against the current implementation in mm/vmalloc.c.

> +Virtual Address Management
> +--------------------------
> +
> +The kernel reserves a large region of virtual address space for vmalloc
> +(on x86-64 this is hundreds of terabytes).  Within this region, allocated
> +and free ranges are tracked by ``struct vmap_area`` nodes organized in two
> +red-black trees — one sorted by address for the busy areas, and one
> +augmented with subtree maximum gap size for the free areas.  The augmented
> +tree allows free-space searches in O(log n) time.

The description of the busy-area tree is inaccurate.  Since the
vmap_node sharding work the busy areas are kept in *per-node*
red-black trees (vn->busy.root inside each struct vmap_node),
not a single global tree.  Only the free-area side remains a
single global augmented rbtree (free_vmap_area_root).  The text
should reflect this N-way sharding and explain why it was done
(to reduce lock contention on vmap_area_root).

> +Guard Pages
> +-----------
> +
> +By default, each vmalloc area is surrounded by a guard page — an unmapped
> +page that causes an immediate fault if code overruns the allocation.

"Surrounded" implies pages on both sides.  The implementation
adds only one guard page (size += PAGE_SIZE in __get_vm_area_node)
at the high end of the allocation.  Adjacent allocations share
that boundary, so there is no separate lower guard page.  Could
you say "followed by a guard page" or "terminated by a guard
page" instead?

> +Huge Page Support
> +-----------------
> +
> +On architectures that support it, vmalloc can use PMD- or PUD-level
> +mappings instead of individual PTEs, reducing TLB pressure for large
> +allocations.

P4D-level mappings are also supported.  vmap_try_huge_p4d() is
called from vmap_pud_range() and guarded by arch_vmap_p4d_supported().
The list should read "PMD-, PUD-, or P4D-level mappings".

> +Freeing
> +=======
> +
> +``vfree()`` can be called from any context, including interrupt handlers.

This is incorrect.  vfree() explicitly forbids NMI context:

    BUG_ON(in_nmi());

The kernel-doc comment for vfree() says:

    "Must not be called in NMI context"

Please correct this to: vfree() can be called from most contexts,
including interrupt handlers, but must not be called from NMI
context.  vfree_atomic() carries the same NMI restriction.

series.title: Docs/mm: document Page Allocation
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Page Allocation

review.summary: Fills in the Documentation/mm/page_allocation.rst stub with a broad overview of the buddy allocator, migratetypes, per-CPU pagesets, GFP flags, the allocation fast/slow paths, watermarks, high-atomic reserves, and compaction. The content is generally accurate but contains two factual errors that should be corrected before merging.
review.review_inline:
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Page Allocation

Good overview of the page allocator subsystem.  Two factual issues
below worth fixing.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/page_allocation.rst b/Documentation/mm/page_allocation.rst

[ ... ]

> +Per-CPU Pagesets
> +================
> +
> +Most order-0 allocations are served from per-CPU page lists (PCP) rather
> +than the global ``free_area``.  This avoids taking the zone lock on the
> +common path, which is critical for scalability on large systems.
> +
> +Each CPU maintains lists of free pages grouped by migratetype.  Pages are
> +moved between the per-CPU lists and the buddy in batches.  The batch size
> +and high watermark for each per-CPU list are tuned based on zone size and
> +the number of CPUs.
> +
> +When a per-CPU list is empty, a batch of pages is taken from the buddy.
> +When it exceeds its high watermark, excess pages are returned.
> +``lru_add_drain()`` and ``drain_all_pages()`` flush per-CPU lists when
> +the system needs an accurate count of free pages, such as during memory
> +hot-remove.

lru_add_drain() and drain_all_pages() are different per-CPU caches.
drain_all_pages() drains the PCP free-page lists (per_cpu_pages).  But
lru_add_drain() drains the LRU pagevec batches (struct cpu_fbatches in
mm/swap.c) -- pending LRU list additions that have not yet been
committed to the LRU proper.  These are unrelated to the PCP free-page
lists described in this section.  Could you either remove lru_add_drain()
from this paragraph, or add a sentence clarifying that it drains a
different per-CPU cache (pending LRU additions) rather than PCP pages?

[ ... ]

> +Memory compaction (``mm/compaction.c``) creates contiguous free blocks for
> +high-order allocations by relocating movable pages.  It runs two scanners
> +across a zone: one walks from the bottom to find movable in-use pages, the
> +other walks from the top to find free pages.  Movable pages are migrated
> +to the free locations, consolidating free space in the middle.

The description of the two scanners is correct, but the final phrase
looks wrong.  The migration scanner starts at the low PFN (bottom) and
walks upward; the free scanner starts at the high PFN (top) and walks
downward.  Movable pages found near the bottom are migrated to the free
slots found near the top.  After compaction, the region formerly
occupied by the migrated pages (the lower portion of the zone) is now
free, while the upper portion is now populated.  Free space ends up at
the bottom of the zone, not in the middle.  The scanners meet in the
middle, but that is the termination condition, not where the free space
concentrates.  Something like "leaving a large contiguous free region at
the lower end of the zone" would be more accurate.

series.title: Docs/mm: document Boot Memory
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document Boot Memory

review.summary: Fills in the Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst stub with an overview of the memblock allocator, its region management, allocation strategy, iterators, and the transition to the buddy allocator. The documentation contains one clear factual error in the CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT description.
review.review_inline:
Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Boot Memory

Good addition overall.  One factual error in the deferred struct-page
initialization section; a few smaller nits below.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst b/Documentation/mm/bootmem.rst

[ ... ]

> +When regions are added, overlapping ranges are merged automatically.
> +Internally, ``memblock_add_range()`` handles insertion, overlap detection,
> +and merging in a single pass.  If the region array is full, it is doubled
> +in size — using memblock itself to allocate the new array.

memblock_double_array() checks slab_is_available() first.  When slab is
already up (which can happen during late boot region additions) it calls
kmalloc() instead.  Worth noting that the fallback is memblock only
during early boot before slab is ready.

[ ... ]

> +With ``CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT``, only pages in the boot node's
> +lower zones are initialized during early boot — enough to get the system
> +running.  The remaining pages are initialized in parallel by worker threads
> +(via the padata framework) before they are first needed.

This description has two inaccuracies worth fixing.

First, the scope is not limited to the "boot node".  defer_init() in
mm/mm_init.c applies the same logic to every NUMA node: lower zones of
all nodes are always initialized eagerly (the comment at line 728 reads
"Always populate low zones for address-constrained allocations"), while
only the highest zone on each node is subject to deferral.

Second, deferred pages are not guaranteed to be initialized "before they
are first needed".  The kthread running deferred_init_memmap() works
through the highest zone via padata, but deferred_grow_zone() also
exists precisely to handle on-demand initialization when an allocation
arrives before the background thread has reached that page-frame range.
So pages may be initialized either in the background or on first demand,
whichever comes first.

A more accurate description: with CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT,
all zones except the highest on each node, plus an initial section of
that highest zone, are initialized synchronously during boot.  The
remaining pages in each node's highest zone are then initialized in
parallel by per-node kthreads using the padata framework, or on demand
via deferred_grow_zone() if an allocation arrives first.

[ ... ]

> +Named Reservations
> +------------------
> +
> +The ``reserve_mem`` kernel command line parameter allows firmware or boot
> +loaders to reserve named memory regions that persist across kexec.

The persistence across kexec is conditional on CONFIG_KEXEC_HANDOVER
(via reserve_mem_kho_revive()).  Without that option the reservations
still work fine for their primary purpose — providing named physical
regions that drivers or subsystems can look up with
reserve_mem_find_by_name() — but they do not survive a kexec.  The
current wording could mislead readers into expecting cross-kexec
behavior unconditionally.

series.title: Docs/mm: document the OOM killer
series.author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs/mm: document the OOM killer

review.summary: This patch fills in the Documentation/mm/oom.rst stub with prose covering OOM victim selection, the OOM reaper, process_mrelease, and sysctl knobs. The content is mostly accurate but contains two factual errors about the reaper's internal API and the totalpages baseline for constrained OOM scenarios.
review.review_inline:
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document the OOM killer

Good start filling in this stub.  A couple of details do not match
the current implementation in mm/oom_kill.c.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/oom.rst b/Documentation/mm/oom.rst

[ ... ]

> +The ``totalpages`` baseline depends on the allocation constraint:
> +
> +- **Unconstrained**: all RAM plus swap.
> +- **Cpuset**: memory on nodes in the current cpuset.
> +- **Memory policy**: memory on nodes in the current mempolicy.
> +- **Memory cgroup**: the cgroup's memory limit.

The Cpuset and Memory policy entries are missing swap.  In
constrained_alloc() both CONSTRAINT_CPUSET and
CONSTRAINT_MEMORY_POLICY seed oc->totalpages with total_swap_pages
before summing node_present_pages() over the constrained nodes:

    oc->totalpages = total_swap_pages;
    for_each_node_mask(nid, cpuset_current_mems_allowed)
        oc->totalpages += node_present_pages(nid);

So those bullets should read something like:

- **Cpuset**: swap plus RAM on nodes in the current cpuset.
- **Memory policy**: swap plus RAM on nodes in the current mempolicy.

> +It walks the victim's VMAs in reverse and calls
> +``unmap_page_range()`` to release physical pages.

The function named here is wrong.  __oom_reap_task_mm() iterates
VMAs with mas_for_each_rev() and calls zap_vma_for_reaping() on
each anonymous or private VMA, not unmap_page_range().
unmap_page_range() is a different helper used elsewhere in the MM
code.

series.title: rust_binder: use AssertSync for BINDER_VM_OPS
series.author: Alice Ryhl <aliceryhl@google.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] rust_binder: use AssertSync for BINDER_VM_OPS

review.summary: Wraps the BINDER_VM_OPS static in AssertSync<vm_operations_struct> to avoid a Sync compilation failure caused by the new pointer field introduced to vm_operations_struct by the userfaultfd vm_uffd_ops patch. The fix follows the existing pattern used for rust_binder_fops.

series.title: mm/vmalloc: free unused pages on vrealloc() shrink
series.author: Shivam Kalra via B4 Relay <devnull+shivamkalra98.zohomail.in@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v4 2/3] mm/vmalloc: free unused pages on vrealloc() shrink

review.summary: The patch implements physical page reclamation on vrealloc() shrink by unmapping and freeing tail pages when a shrink crosses a page boundary, and fixes a latent grow-in-place bug where post-shrink grows would reference already-freed pages.
review.review_inline:
From: Shivam Kalra <shivamkalra98@zohomail.in>

mm/vmalloc: free unused pages on vrealloc() shrink

Overall the direction is correct and the grow-in-place fix is clearly
necessary.  A few things worth checking:

> diff --git a/mm/vmalloc.c b/mm/vmalloc.c
> --- a/mm/vmalloc.c
> +++ b/mm/vmalloc.c

[ ... ]

> +		/* Free tail pages when shrink crosses a page boundary. */
> +		if (new_nr_pages < vm->nr_pages && !vm_area_page_order(vm)) {
> +			unsigned long addr = (unsigned long)p;
> +
> +			vunmap_range(addr + (new_nr_pages << PAGE_SHIFT),
> +				     addr + (vm->nr_pages << PAGE_SHIFT));
> +
> +			vm_area_free_pages(vm, new_nr_pages, vm->nr_pages);
> +			vm->nr_pages = new_nr_pages;
> +		}

vfree() checks (vm->flags & VM_FLUSH_RESET_PERMS) and, if set, calls
vm_reset_perms() which iterates over vm->nr_pages pages to reset their
direct-map permissions before releasing them to the buddy allocator.
After the partial free updates vm->nr_pages = new_nr_pages, a subsequent
vfree() will call vm_reset_perms() only on the surviving pages; the
tail pages freed here would have been released to the buddy allocator
without having their direct-map permissions restored.

Does vm_area_free_pages() (introduced in patch 1/3) call
set_area_direct_map() or otherwise reset direct-map permissions for
the freed pages when VM_FLUSH_RESET_PERMS is set?  Or should there be
a guard here:

    if (vm->flags & VM_FLUSH_RESET_PERMS)
        goto skip_partial_free;

to skip the optimisation for allocations that need the direct-map
reset path in vfree()?

Similarly, vfree() calls mod_lruvec_page_state(page, NR_VMALLOC, -1)
for each freed page (unless VM_MAP_PUT_PAGES is set).  Can you confirm
that vm_area_free_pages() mirrors that accounting for the freed tail
pages, so NR_VMALLOC stays accurate?

> +		vm->nr_pages = new_nr_pages;

After this update vm->pages[new_nr_pages..old_nr_pages-1] still hold
the (now-freed) page pointers.  vfree() won't touch them because it
stops at vm->nr_pages, so there is no double-free, but should
vm_area_free_pages() NULL those slots out so that any future debugging
visit (e.g. /proc/vmallocinfo or a crash dump) does not see stale
pointers?

[ ... ]

> -	if (size <= alloced_size) {
> +	if (size <= (size_t)vm->nr_pages << PAGE_SHIFT) {

The grow-in-place fix is correct.  alloced_size captures
get_vm_area_size(vm) once at entry; after a shrink reduces
vm->nr_pages, the old comparison would have allowed a grow into
already-freed pages.  Comparing against the live vm->nr_pages value
prevents that.

series.title: binfmt_elf: Align eligible read-only PT_LOAD segments to PMD_SIZE for THP
series.author: WANG Rui <r@hev.cc>

patches.title: [PATCH v5] binfmt_elf: Align eligible read-only PT_LOAD segments to PMD_SIZE for THP

review.summary: The patch adds a helper should_align_to_pmd() that bumps p_align to PMD_SIZE for eligible read-only, PMD-aligned PT_LOAD segments in maximum_alignment(), so that the computed load_bias for ET_DYN binaries is PMD-aligned, enabling THP for large text segments. The code is correct but the commit message has a subtle misleading description worth clarifying.
review.review_inline:
WANG Rui <r@hev.cc>

binfmt_elf: Align eligible read-only PT_LOAD segments to PMD_SIZE for THP

The optimization looks mechanically correct.  A few things worth
clarifying:

> +static inline bool should_align_to_pmd(const struct elf_phdr *cmd)
> +{
> +	if (!IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE))
> +		return false;
> +
> +	/*
> +	 * Avoid excessive virtual address space padding when PMD_SIZE is very
> +	 * large, since this function increases PT_LOAD alignment.
> +	 * This threshold roughly matches the largest commonly used hugepage
> +	 * sizes on current architectures (e.g. x86 2M, arm64 32M with 16K pages).
> +	 */
> +	if (PMD_SIZE > SZ_32M)
> +		return false;
> +
> +	if (!IS_ALIGNED(cmd->p_vaddr | cmd->p_offset, PMD_SIZE))
> +		return false;

The eligibility criteria in the commit message list "both p_vaddr and
p_offset are PMD-aligned" as a prerequisite, but the motivation section
says "the virtual address is not aligned due to the small p_align
value."  These appear contradictory to a reader who doesn't already
know the answer.

The distinction is that p_vaddr here is the ELF-relative address (which
IS PMD-aligned in the binary), while "the virtual address" in the
motivation refers to the final load address (load_bias + p_vaddr), which
is NOT PMD-aligned because load_bias is not PMD-aligned when p_align is
small.  Could the commit message spell this out, e.g.: "p_vaddr is
already PMD-aligned in the ELF file, but the final virtual address
(load_bias + p_vaddr) is not, because load_bias is only aligned to the
binary's p_align"?

[ ... ]

> +			if (p_align < PMD_SIZE && should_align_to_pmd(&cmds[i]))
> +				p_align = PMD_SIZE;
> +
> 			alignment = max(alignment, p_align);

maximum_alignment() is only called in the ET_DYN branch of
load_elf_binary() (inside the first-PT_LOAD handling for ET_DYN),
not for ET_EXEC.  For ET_EXEC binaries load_bias is zero and
segments are mapped at their absolute p_vaddr, so this path is
never reached for them.

The commit message says "This only affects ELF executables loaded
directly by the kernel ELF binary loader" without distinguishing
ET_DYN from ET_EXEC.  Could it note that the benefit is limited to
PIE (ET_DYN) executables?  The benchmark already uses --enable-host-pie
so the numbers are consistent, but the prose could be clearer.

series.title: zswap pool per-CPU acomp_ctx simplifications

patches.title: [PATCH 0/2] zswap pool per-CPU acomp_ctx simplifications
[PATCH 1/2] mm: zswap: Tie per-CPU acomp_ctx lifetime to the pool.
[PATCH 2/2] mm: zswap: Consistently use IS_ERR_OR_NULL() to check acomp_ctx resources.

The lifetime simplification is sound, but Patch 2 introduces a latent PTR_ERR(NULL)==0 bug in the failure path of zswap_cpu_comp_prepare().

series.title: fixup: mm/memory: inline unmap_page_range() into __zap_vma_range()
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH] mm/damon/stat: fix wrong argument to damon_set_regions()
[PATCH] mm/damon/core: fix wrong damon_set_regions() argument
[PATCH] tmp
[PATCH] tmp
[PATCH] fixup: mm/memory: inline unmap_page_range() into __zap_vma_range()
[PATCH 1/1] userfaultfd: allow registration of WP_ASYNC for any VMA

review.summary: This series bundles two damon/damon-core bug fixes (wrong nr_ranges argument to damon_set_regions), a khugepaged refactoring to extract max_ptes helper functions, a pagewalk PUD-validity race fix, a cosmetic memory.c variable rename, and a userfaultfd WP_ASYNC registration fix. The damon and userfaultfd fixes are straightforwardly correct; the khugepaged and pagewalk patches need attention.
review.review_inline:
Series: fixup: mm/memory: inline unmap_page_range() into __zap_vma_range()

--- [PATCH] mm/damon/stat: fix wrong argument to damon_set_regions() ---

> -	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, addr_unit, min_region_sz);
> +	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, 1, min_region_sz);

Good catch.  damon_set_regions() iterates 0..nr_ranges over the
ranges[] array, so passing addr_unit (e.g. PAGE_SIZE = 4096) as the
count would walk far off the end of the single-element &addr_range.
Fixing it to 1 is correct.

--- [PATCH] mm/damon/core: fix wrong damon_set_regions() argument ---

> -	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, addr_unit, min_region_sz);
> +	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, 1, min_region_sz);

Same fix in damon_set_region_biggest_system_ram_default().  Correct.

--- [PATCH] tmp (mm/khugepaged.c refactoring) ---

The function names in this diff -- hpage_collapse_scan_pmd() and
hpage_collapse_scan_file() -- do not match the current mm-new tree,
which has already renamed these to collapse_scan_pmd() and
collapse_scan_file().  The patch will not apply cleanly and needs
rebasing.

Assuming the semantic intent:

> +static int collapse_max_ptes_none(struct collapse_control *cc,
> +		struct vm_area_struct *vma)
> +{
> +	/* We don't mess with MISSING faults. */
> +	if (vma && userfaultfd_armed(vma))
> +		return 0;
> +	/* MADV_COLLAPSE always collapses. */
> +	if (!cc->is_khugepaged)
> +		return HPAGE_PMD_NR;
> +	return khugepaged_max_ptes_none;
> +}

For hpage_collapse_scan_file (collapse_scan_file in the tree), the
call is collapse_max_ptes_none(cc, NULL).  With vma=NULL the
userfaultfd_armed() branch is skipped, which matches the original
code (no uffd check existed for file collapse).  OK.

> +		if (++none_or_zero > max_ptes_none) {

For MADV_COLLAPSE without userfaultfd, max_ptes_none == HPAGE_PMD_NR,
so the condition is never true across HPAGE_PMD_NR iterations.  Same
effect as the original !cc->is_khugepaged short-circuit.  Verified
equivalent for all three callers and all combinations of
cc->is_khugepaged and userfaultfd_armed(vma).

> +		if (++unmapped > max_ptes_swap) {
> +			/*
> +			 * Always be strict with uffd-wp enabled swap entries.

For MADV_COLLAPSE, max_ptes_swap == HPAGE_PMD_NR; unmapped can never
exceed HPAGE_PMD_NR, so the threshold check is effectively skipped,
same as the original !cc->is_khugepaged guard.  The uffd-wp check
that was inside the old "allowed" branch is now placed after the
threshold check, which is correct since it is only reached when
unmapped <= max_ptes_swap.

The refactoring looks semantically equivalent throughout after rebasing.

--- [PATCH] tmp (mm/pagewalk.c) ---

> +static int walk_pmd_range(pud_t *pud, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,
> +			  struct mm_walk *walk)
> +{
> +	pud_t pudval = pudp_get(pud);

[ ... ]

> +	if (!pud_present(pudval) || pud_leaf(pudval)) {
> +		walk->action = ACTION_AGAIN;
> +		return 0;
> +	}

The intent is sound: by snapshotting the PUD before touching PMD
offsets we can detect that split_huge_pud() cleared the entry (or a
concurrent unmap beat us), and signal walk_pud_range() to retry via
ACTION_AGAIN rather than dereference a stale PMD table pointer.

One thing worth noting: after the pudp_get() check passes,
pmd_offset() is still called with the live pud pointer and
internally re-reads *pud.  If the entry is concurrently cleared
between pudp_get() and pmd_offset(), the walk could compute a bogus
PMD base.  This race exists in the pre-patch code too and is
presumably ruled out by the held mmap lock.  A comment alongside the
new check explaining that assumption (parallel to the comment already
written above it for the PTE case) would help future readers.

> -		if (pud_none(*pud))
> -			goto again;
> -
>  		err = walk_pmd_range(pud, addr, next, walk);
>  		if (err)
>  			break;
> +
> +		if (walk->action == ACTION_AGAIN)
> +			goto again;

Removing the old pud_none() check is safe here because:
  1. In the !walk->vma path, pud_none after the else-if guard was
     unreachable (a non-leaf, present PUD cannot simultaneously be
     none).
  2. In the walk->vma path (after split_huge_pud), the new early
     check inside walk_pmd_range catches !pud_present via ACTION_AGAIN.

The check !pud_present(pudval) || pud_leaf(pudval) is strictly broader
than the old pud_none(), which is correct: a huge PUD that split_huge_pud
failed to split should also trigger a retry.

walk_pmd_range() is the only caller in pagewalk.c, and the vmscan.c
function of the same name is unrelated (different signature, local to
vmscan.c).  No other call sites are affected.

--- [PATCH] fixup: mm/memory: rename cur to addr ---

Already applied in the tree.  Cosmetic only, no issues.

--- [PATCH 1/1] userfaultfd: allow registration of WP_ASYNC for any VMA ---

Already applied in the tree.  The reordering -- moving the wp_async
check before the !ops guard -- correctly allows SysV shm VMAs (which
have no vm_uffd_ops) to register for WP_ASYNC write-protect tracking
without needing VMA-specific fault resolution.  VM_DROPPABLE is no
longer checked for this path, which is acceptable because WP_ASYNC
faults are handled entirely by the generic page fault path and do not
require vm_uffd_ops.

series.title: selftests/mm: add folio_split() and filemap_get_entry() race test
series.author: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] selftests/mm: add folio_split() and filemap_get_entry() race test

review.summary: Adds a C userspace selftest that races folio_split() against filemap_get_entry() by having a parent process punch holes via MADV_REMOVE into a shmem file while 16 child threads continuously read and verify the non-punched pages. This is a port of an existing Rust test.
review.review_inline:
Author: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>

selftests/mm: add folio_split() and filemap_get_entry() race test

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/folio_split_race_test.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/folio_split_race_test.c
> new file mode 100644
> --- /dev/null
> +++ b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/folio_split_race_test.c

[ ... ]

> +static int get_errno(void)
> +{
> +	return errno;
> +}

This static helper is not referenced anywhere in the visible code above
run_iteration().  With CFLAGS = -Wall the compiler will emit an
unused-function warning.  Could you either use it in place of a bare
errno read somewhere, or remove it?

[ ... ]

> +	while (atomic_load_explicit(&ctl->stop, memory_order_acquire) == 0) {
> +		for (page_idx = (size_t)tid; page_idx < TOTAL_PAGES;
> +		     page_idx += NUM_READER_THREADS) {
> +			if (page_idx % PUNCH_INTERVAL >= 0 &&
> +			    page_idx % PUNCH_INTERVAL < PUNCH_SIZE_FACTOR)
> +				continue;

page_idx is size_t (unsigned).  The sub-expression
(page_idx % PUNCH_INTERVAL >= 0) is always true, so the first half of
the conjunction is dead.  GCC/Clang both warn about this under -Wall
(-Wtype-limits), and the Makefile already sets -Wall.  The same pattern
appears again in child_reader_loop().  The fix is to drop the >= 0 half:

			if (page_idx % PUNCH_INTERVAL < PUNCH_SIZE_FACTOR)
				continue;

Also, the modulo is computed twice; a local variable would make it
clearer and avoid the redundant operation.

[ ... ]

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/run_vmtests.sh b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/run_vmtests.sh

The diff header shows +2 lines but they are not reproduced here.  Could
you confirm that a new category is listed and documented in the usage()
function?  Looking at the existing script, every category that is passed
to run_test via CATEGORY= is expected to appear in the usage() category
description list, otherwise automated CI tooling will not pick it up
correctly.  If the test is added under the existing "thp" category that
should be fine as-is, but a brief note in the usage() block would help.

series.title: pid_namespace: make init creation more flexible
series.author: Pavel Tikhomirov <ptikhomirov@virtuozzo.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v5 0/4] pid_namespace: make init creation more flexible
[PATCH v5 1/4] pid_namespace: avoid optimization of accesses to ->child_reaper
[PATCH v5 2/4] pid: check init is created first after idr alloc
[PATCH v5 3/4] pid_namespace: allow opening pid_for_children before init was created
[PATCH v5 4/4] selftests: Add tests for creating pidns init via setns

review.summary: This series allows a pid namespace to be joined via setns() before its init process is created, enabling CRIU-style restoration of nested containers with clone3(set_tid). It does so by properly annotating child_reaper accesses with WRITE_ONCE/READ_ONCE, enforcing "init must be PID 1" at IDR-alloc time, and removing the child_reaper gate from pidns_for_children_get().
review.review_inline:
Pavel Tikhomirov <ptikhomirov@virtuozzo.com>

pid_namespace: make init creation more flexible

Patches 1-3 look correct overall. One annotation inconsistency in
patch 1 is worth fixing before merging.

> diff --git a/kernel/pid.c b/kernel/pid.c
> --- a/kernel/pid.c
> +++ b/kernel/pid.c
> @@ -128,7 +128,7 @@ void free_pid(struct pid *pid)
>  			 * is the reaper wake up the reaper.  The reaper
>  			 * may be sleeping in zap_pid_ns_processes().
>  			 */
> -			wake_up_process(ns->child_reaper);
> +			wake_up_process(READ_ONCE(ns->child_reaper));
>  			break;
>  		case PIDNS_ADDING:
>  			/* Handle a fork failure of the first process */
> -			WARN_ON(ns->child_reaper);

The WARN_ON on the very next line reads ns->child_reaper as a plain
access, without READ_ONCE.  Both the case-1/2 path and the
PIDNS_ADDING path are under only pidmap_lock, not tasklist_lock, so
neither read is protected against the writers that this patch now
annotates with WRITE_ONCE.  With WRITE_ONCE on the store side, KCSAN
will flag the WARN_ON's plain load as a data race.  Could you add
READ_ONCE there as well, consistent with what the patch does for
wake_up_process just above?

[ ... ]

> diff --git a/kernel/pid.c b/kernel/pid.c
> @@ -293,9 +293,18 @@ struct pid *alloc_pid(struct pid_namespace *ns, pid_t *arg_set_tid,
> +		/*
> +		 * PID 1 (init) must be created first.
> +		 */
> +		if (!READ_ONCE(tmp->child_reaper) && nr != 1) {
> +			retval = -EINVAL;
> +			goto out_free;
> +		}

The goto out_free here correctly undoes exactly the IDR entries
allocated so far (++i in the while loop recovers the current level
before tmp = tmp->parent advances it), and the PIDNS_ADDING cursor
reset at out_free only touches the innermost ns, which is the only
level where child_reaper can be NULL in practice.  Looks correct.

The READ_ONCE here is under pidmap_lock only, not tasklist_lock;
the WRITE_ONCE in copy_process is under tasklist write lock.  As
Oleg noted, a CPU on a weakly-ordered architecture could in theory
read a stale NULL even after child_reaper is set on another CPU,
but the consequence is a spurious -EINVAL that the caller can retry,
not a safety hazard.  The WRITE_ONCE/READ_ONCE pairing is the
right annotation for this lockless cross-lock read.

[ ... ]

> diff --git a/kernel/pid_namespace.c b/kernel/pid_namespace.c
> @@ -369,15 +369,6 @@ static struct ns_common *pidns_for_children_get
> -	if (ns) {
> -		read_lock(&tasklist_lock);
> -		if (!ns->child_reaper) {
> -			put_pid_ns(ns);
> -			ns = NULL;
> -		}
> -		read_unlock(&tasklist_lock);
> -	}

Removing this gate is the heart of the series.  Safety depends on
the alloc_pid() check in patch 2 rejecting any non-1 PID when
child_reaper is NULL, and on child_reaper never being cleared back
to NULL once it is set (find_child_reaper only replaces it with
another live task, never with NULL; reboot_pid_ns and
zap_pid_ns_processes both require init to be alive).

I verified that alloc_pid's out_free path correctly removes the IDR
entry and, when pid_allocated == PIDNS_ADDING, resets the cursor so
the next fork retries from PID 1.  The race where two processes
compete to become init is handled correctly: the second gets -EEXIST
(set_tid) or -EINVAL (non-set_tid, nr != 1) from alloc_pid and must
retry.  This is intentional and documented in the commit message.

series.title: Refactor reserved memory regions handling code
series.author: Marek Szyprowski <m.szyprowski@samsung.com>

patches.title: [PATCH 0/7] Refactor reserved memory regions handling code
[PATCH 1/7] of: reserved_mem: remove fdt node from the structure
[PATCH 2/7] of: reserved_mem: use -ENODEV instead of -ENOENT
[PATCH 3/7] of: reserved_mem: switch to ops based OF_DECLARE()
[PATCH 4/7] of: reserved_mem: replace CMA quirks by generic methods
[PATCH 5/7] of: reserved_mem: rearrange code a bit
[PATCH 6/7] of: reserved_mem: clarify fdt_scan_reserved_mem*() functions
[PATCH 7/7] of: reserved_mem: rework fdt_init_reserved_mem_node()

review.summary: This 7-patch series refactors reserved memory region handling in drivers/of/of_reserved_mem.c, removing the fdt_node struct field, introducing ops-based RESERVEDMEM_OF_DECLARE(), and replacing CMA-specific quirks with generic node_validate/node_fixup callbacks. Two functional bugs were found: a broken stub macro in the !CONFIG_OF_RESERVED_MEM path, and a wrong local variable passed to fdt_validate_reserved_mem_node() inside fdt_scan_reserved_mem_late() that silently disables CMA default-region skipping for statically-defined nodes.
review.review_inline:
Marek Szyprowski <m.szyprowski@samsung.com>

of: reserved_mem: refactor reserved memory regions handling code

[ ... ]

--- [PATCH 3/7] of: reserved_mem: switch to ops based OF_DECLARE() ---

> diff --git a/include/linux/of_reserved_mem.h b/include/linux/of_reserved_mem.h
> --- a/include/linux/of_reserved_mem.h
> +++ b/include/linux/of_reserved_mem.h

[ ... ]

> +#define RESERVEDMEM_OF_DECLARE(name, compat, ops)			\
> +	_OF_DECLARE_STUB(reservedmem, name, compat, init, void *)

The stub branch (CONFIG_OF_RESERVED_MEM not set) still refers to
'init', which is not a parameter of this macro.  The macro parameter
was renamed from 'init' to 'ops' in the enabled branch, but the
disabled branch was not updated.  Expanding _OF_DECLARE_STUB with an
undefined identifier 'init' in place of 'fn' will produce a
compilation error any time a driver calls RESERVEDMEM_OF_DECLARE()
with CONFIG_OF_RESERVED_MEM disabled.  Should this read:

  _OF_DECLARE_STUB(reservedmem, name, compat, ops, void *)

--- [PATCH 4/7] of: reserved_mem: replace CMA quirks by generic methods ---

> diff --git a/drivers/of/of_reserved_mem.c b/drivers/of/of_reserved_mem.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -251,17 +251,19 @@ void __init fdt_scan_reserved_mem_reg_nodes(void)
>  
>  	fdt_for_each_subnode(child, fdt, node) {
>  		const char *uname;
> -		bool default_cma = of_get_flat_dt_prop(child, "linux,cma-default", NULL);
>  		u64 b, s;
> +		int ret;
>  
>  		if (!of_fdt_device_is_available(fdt, child))
>  			continue;
> -		if (default_cma && cma_skip_dt_default_reserved_mem())
> -			continue;
>  
>  		if (!of_flat_dt_get_addr_size(child, "reg", &b, &s))
>  			continue;
>  
> +		ret = fdt_validate_reserved_mem_node(node, NULL);
> +		if (ret && ret != -ENODEV)
> +			continue;

This passes 'node' (the parent /reserved-memory node) instead of
'child' (the subnode currently being visited) to
fdt_validate_reserved_mem_node().  Inside fdt_for_each_subnode(child,
fdt, node), 'node' is the result of fdt_path_offset(fdt,
"/reserved-memory").  The parent node does not carry the
"shared-dma-pool" or "linux,cma-default" properties, so
of_flat_dt_is_compatible() never matches and
fdt_validate_reserved_mem_node() always returns -ENODEV.  The guard
'if (ret && ret != -ENODEV)' therefore never fires.

The CMA default-region skip logic -- which the old code expressed as:

  bool default_cma = of_get_flat_dt_prop(child, "linux,cma-default", NULL);
  if (default_cma && cma_skip_dt_default_reserved_mem())
      continue;

-- is completely bypassed for statically-placed ("reg") reserved memory
nodes.  A board that defines its CMA region via a DT "reg" property
and also passes "cma=" on the kernel command line will now attempt to
initialize the region twice instead of honoring the command-line
override.

Should this read fdt_validate_reserved_mem_node(child, NULL) ?

series.title: arm64/mm: contpte-sized exec folios for 16K and 64K pages
series.author: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

patches.title: [PATCH 0/4] arm64/mm: contpte-sized exec folios for 16K and 64K pages
[PATCH 1/4] arm64: request contpte-sized folios for exec memory
[PATCH 2/4] mm: bypass mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead
[PATCH 3/4] elf: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping
[PATCH 4/4] mm: align file-backed mmap to exec folio order in thp_get_unmapped_area

review.summary: This series enables contpte-sized exec folios on arm64 16K/64K page configs by fixing exec_folio_order(), bypassing the mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead, aligning PIE load bias to folio size, and adding a folio-size VA alignment fallback in thp_get_unmapped_area. The core approach is sound but patch 2 introduces a regression for VM_EXEC+VM_HUGEPAGE VMAs, and patch 3 has an unintended side effect on ET_DYN binaries without PT_INTERP.

series.title: elf: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping
series.author: WANG Rui <r@hev.cc>

patches.title: [PATCH 3/4] elf: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping

review.summary: The patch bumps the ET_DYN load alignment to exec_folio_order page size so that PIE binaries land on contpte-eligible boundaries on arm64, but it does so unconditionally for every ET_DYN binary regardless of whether the LOAD segments are actually positioned to benefit from contpte folding.
review.review_inline:
WANG Rui <r@hev.cc>

ell: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping

> +			if (exec_folio_order())
> +				alignment = max(alignment,
> +					(unsigned long)PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order());

The ASLR entropy concern you raise is the central one here.  On arm64
with 4K base pages exec_folio_order() is 4, so this forces a minimum
64K alignment for every ET_DYN binary.  In the PT_INTERP path that
translates directly to masking 4 more bits out of arch_mmap_rnd()
for every PIE executable on the system, not just the ones that
actually benefit.

For the optimization to produce a contpte-eligible mapping, three
things must all be true simultaneously:

  1. p_vaddr of the LOAD segment is 64K-aligned (so load_bias + vaddr
     lands on a 64K boundary after the masking below).
  2. p_offset of the LOAD segment is 64K-aligned (so the file-backed
     mmap starts at a 64K-aligned page-cache offset).
  3. The segment is large enough to contain at least one full contpte
     block.

If condition 1 is not met, masking load_bias to 64K still leaves
(load_bias + vaddr) unaligned, so no contpte folding happens and the
ALSR entropy was sacrificed for nothing.  Similarly for condition 2.
A binary produced by a linker that only guarantees 4K p_align gets
the full ASLR penalty with no iTLB benefit.

There is also a side-effect on ET_DYN binaries without PT_INTERP
(static PIE, typically the dynamic linker itself).  Previously, if
maximum_alignment() returned PAGE_SIZE, the condition

    if (alignment > ELF_MIN_ALIGN)

was false and load_bias stayed 0, leaving address selection entirely
to the kernel mmap path.  With this change, alignment is bumped to
64K on arm64, which is always > ELF_MIN_ALIGN (PAGE_SIZE), so every
static PIE binary now goes through the probe-map / vm_munmap /
MAP_FIXED_NOREPLACE sequence even when its segments carry only 4K
alignment in their headers and contpte folding will not occur.  Is
that extra overhead intentional for this class of binary?

Finally, exec_folio_order() is evaluated twice in a single
statement.  Both the generic fallback and the arm64 definition are
constant, so there is no runtime cost, but storing the result in a
local makes the code easier to read and avoids any concern if a
future architecture provides a non-trivial implementation.

    unsigned int folio_order = exec_folio_order();
    if (folio_order)
        alignment = max(alignment,
                        (unsigned long)PAGE_SIZE << folio_order);

Suggested constraint, along the lines of what you describe: only
bump the alignment when at least one PT_LOAD segment has both
p_vaddr and p_offset 64K-aligned and p_filesz >= 64K.  That would
confine the ASLR cost to binaries that were actually linked with
contpte-eligible layout, while leaving everything else unchanged.

series.title: selftests/mm: skip several tests when thp is not available
series.author: Chunyu Hu <chuhu@redhat.com>

patches.title: [PATCH 0/4] selftests/mm: skip several tests when thp is not available

review.summary: The series adds THP availability guards to four mm selftests (guard-regions collapse, soft-dirty hugepage, split_huge_page_test, and transhuge-stress) to prevent false positives on kernels compiled without THP support, such as real-time kernels.
review.review_inline:
Author: Chunyu Hu <chuhu@redhat.com>

selftests/mm: skip several tests when thp is not available

The series goal is correct: these tests produce false positives on
kernels with THP disabled.  A few implementation details are worth
discussing.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/soft-dirty.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/soft-dirty.c

[ ... ]

> +	if (!thp_is_enabled()) {
> +		ksft_test_result_skip("Transparent Hugepages not available\n");
> +		return;
> +	}

test_hugepage() always emits two ksft_test_result calls: one for
"huge page allocation" and one for "huge page dirty bit" (it
emits two skips in the else branch when check_huge_anon() fails).
The new early return only emits one skip, so the total result count
will be 18 instead of the planned 19, causing ksft_finished() to
report a plan mismatch (it checks plan == pass + xfail + xskip).
The cover letter says "skip two tests" -- should there be a second
ksft_test_result_skip call here before the return?

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/guard-regions.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/guard-regions.c

[ ... ]

> +	if (!thp_is_enabled()) {
> +		TEST_RESULT_SKIP(...);
> +		return;
> +	}

(quoting the likely form of the guard)

The collapse test uses MADV_COLLAPSE which is a per-VMA request and
operates independently of the global THP policy.  thp_is_enabled()
returns false when THP is compiled in but the system policy is set
to "never", which would skip the test on kernels where MADV_COLLAPSE
would work fine.  Would thp_available() be more appropriate here?
thp_available() only returns false when the THP sysfs interface is
absent, i.e. THP was not compiled in at all.  The distinction
matters for the collapse test since the madvise call itself is what
you're testing, not whether the khugepaged or automatic promotion
path is active.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/split_huge_page_test.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/split_huge_page_test.c

[ ... ]

> -	if (!pmd_pagesize)
> -		ksft_exit_fail_msg("Reading PMD pagesize failed\n");
> +	if (!pmd_pagesize)
> +		ksft_exit_skip("Transparent Hugepages not available\n");

The check for pmd_pagesize == 0 comes at line 783-785, after
ksft_print_header() but before ksft_set_plan().  ksft_exit_skip()
prints "1..0 # SKIP" in that state (ksft_plan is 0), so the TAP
output should be valid.  The 9-line addition suggests multiple
change sites -- are any of them after the ksft_set_plan() call?  If
ksft_exit_skip() is reached after the plan is already printed, the
output will instead produce a spurious "ok N # SKIP" which confuses
some TAP consumers.

series.title: mm/damon: support addr_unit on default monitoring targets for modules
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH 0/5] mm/damon: support addr_unit on default monitoring targets for modules
[PATCH 1/5] mm/damon/core: fix wrong end address assignment on walk_system_ram()
[PATCH 2/5] mm/damon/core: support addr_unit on damon_find_biggest_system_ram()
[PATCH 3/5] mm/damon/core: receive addr_unit on damon_set_region_biggest_system_ram_default()
[PATCH 4/5] mm/damon/reclaim: respect addr_unit on default monitoring region setup
[PATCH 5/5] mm/damon/lru_sort: respect addr_unit on default monitoring region setup

review.summary: This series extends addr_unit support to the default system RAM monitoring target setup functions in DAMON core, DAMON_RECLAIM, and DAMON_LRU_SORT, and fixes a pre-existing off-by-one in walk_system_ram(). Patch 3 introduces a critical bug by passing addr_unit as the nr_ranges argument to damon_set_regions(), causing out-of-bounds stack reads when addr_unit > 1.
review.review_inline:
SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon: support addr_unit on default monitoring targets for modules

The goal of this series is clear and the overall design is sound.  Two
issues found below.

> diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c
> index 3925720a172a6..aee61bf991baa 100644
> --- a/mm/damon/core.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/core.c

[ ... patch 2 ... ]

> +static bool damon_find_biggest_system_ram(unsigned long *start,
> +		unsigned long *end, unsigned long addr_unit)
> +
> +{
> +	struct resource res = {};
> +
> +	walk_system_ram_res(0, -1, &res, walk_system_ram);
> +	if (res.end < res.start)
> +		return false;

The "no system RAM found" sentinel is broken here.  The old code
used a zeroed damon_addr_range and checked arg.end <= arg.start,
which correctly caught the {start=0, end=0} initial state (0 <= 0
is true -> return false).  The new code initialises a zeroed struct
resource (res.start=0, res.end=0) and checks res.end < res.start
(0 < 0 is false), so if walk_system_ram_res never calls the
callback the function does not return false.  It instead computes
*start = 0 and *end = 1 (for addr_unit=1) and returns true,
leading the caller to set up a spurious [0, 1) monitoring region.

The condition should be res.end < res.start || (!res.start && !res.end),
or the initial sentinel for res should be chosen so that a later
"< " comparison can distinguish it from a valid zero-start region.

[ ... ]

> diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c
> index aee61bf991baa..d4f86c20b4f48 100644
> --- a/mm/damon/core.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/core.c

[ ... patch 3 ... ]

> -	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, 1, min_region_sz);
> +	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, addr_unit, min_region_sz);

This passes addr_unit as the nr_ranges argument to damon_set_regions(),
but nr_ranges is the count of elements in the ranges[] array, not
the address granularity.  The array here is always exactly one
element (the single addr_range on the stack).  The correct third
argument is 1, not addr_unit.

With addr_unit > 1 (e.g. a user setting addr_unit to PAGE_SIZE),
damon_set_regions() iterates its two inner loops addr_unit times:

  for (i = 0; i < nr_ranges; i++) {
      if (damon_intersect(r, &ranges[i]))  /* ranges[1..addr_unit-1] OOB */

and

  for (i = 0; i < nr_ranges; i++) {
      range = &ranges[i];                  /* ranges[1..addr_unit-1] OOB */
      ...
      newr = damon_new_region(
          ALIGN_DOWN(range->start, min_region_sz),
          ALIGN(range->end, min_region_sz));

both of which read from stack memory beyond addr_range, leading to
corrupt region start/end values being installed in the monitoring
target, or, if damon_intersect fires on garbage values, to spurious
region deletion.

The line should remain:
  return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, 1, min_region_sz);

[PATCH v6 2/4] mm: huge_memory: refactor anon_enabled_store() with change_anon_orders()

review.summary: The series avoids redundant start_stop_khugepaged() calls by tracking whether THP mode bits actually changed, and ratelimits the resulting log messages. The logic is mostly correct but set_global_enabled_mode() drops SMP-safe atomicity without adding a compensating lock.
review.review_inline:
Breno Leitao <leitao@debian.org>

mm: thp: reduce unnecessary start_stop_khugepaged() calls

The motivation is clear and the overall approach is sound.  One
concern with patch 3 that is worth discussing below.

> diff --git a/mm/huge_memory.c b/mm/huge_memory.c

[ ... patches 1 and 2 look correct ... ]

> --- [PATCH v6 3/4] mm: huge_memory: refactor enabled_store() with change_enabled() ---

> +static bool set_global_enabled_mode(enum global_enabled_mode mode)
> +{
> +	static const unsigned long thp_flags[] = {
> +		TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE_FLAG,
> +		TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE_REQ_MADV_FLAG,
> +	};
> +	enum global_enabled_mode m;
> +	bool changed = false;
> +
> +	for (m = 0; m < ARRAY_SIZE(thp_flags); m++) {
> +		if (m == mode)
> +			changed |= !__test_and_set_bit(thp_flags[m],
> +						       &transparent_hugepage_flags);
> +		else
> +			changed |= __test_and_clear_bit(thp_flags[m],
> +						&transparent_hugepage_flags);
> +	}
> +
> +	return changed;
> +}

The original enabled_store() used clear_bit() and set_bit() on
transparent_hugepage_flags -- those are SMP-safe (LOCK-prefixed on
x86).  This new helper replaces them with __test_and_set_bit() and
__test_and_clear_bit(), which are the non-atomic variants, and holds
no lock around the loop.

The commit description says this uses "the same loop pattern as
set_anon_enabled_mode()", but set_anon_enabled_mode() protects its
non-atomic bit operations with spin_lock(&huge_anon_orders_lock).
set_global_enabled_mode() has no such protection.  Two concurrent
sysfs writes could race on transparent_hugepage_flags at the bit
level with no serialization at all, which is weaker than the
original code.

Would it make sense to either hold a lock (mirroring
set_anon_enabled_mode) or use the atomic test_and_set_bit() /
test_and_clear_bit() variants here instead?

[ ... rest of patch 3 and patch 4 look correct ... ]

review.summary: A treewide mechanical cleanup replacing 'IS_ERR() && NULL' guard patterns with IS_ERR_OR_NULL(), generated via Coccinelle. The mm/kmemleak.c changes are correct, but patches 55-58 introduce a semantic change where NULL now triggers WARN_ON/WARN_ON_ONCE in cleanup functions that are documented to accept NULL as a no-op.
review.review_inline:
Author: Philipp Hahn <phahn-oss@avm.de>

treewide: Use IS_ERR_OR_NULL over manual NULL check - refactor

The mm/kmemleak.c conversions (patch 33) look correct. The reordering in
kmemleak_scan_area() from (ptr && size) to (size && !IS_ERR_OR_NULL(ptr))
is safe: ptr is not dereferenced before the NULL check is performed, and
IS_ERR_OR_NULL() subsumes the original ptr-truthiness test.

The main concern is with patches 55-58, which the author already flags.

--- [PATCH 01/61] Coccinelle ---

> +/// Use IF_ERR_OR_NULL() instead of IS_ERR() plus a check for (not) NULL

Typo in the script header: "IF_ERR_OR_NULL" should be "IS_ERR_OR_NULL".

--- [PATCH 55/61] interconnect ---

> -	if (!path || WARN_ON(IS_ERR(path)))
> +	if (WARN_ON(IS_ERR_OR_NULL(path)))
>  		return;

The interconnect API allows icc_get() to return NULL for optional paths
that are not present in the device tree.  Callers that use optional ICC
paths will call icc_put(NULL) during cleanup, which was silently ignored
before.  With this change, every such call site would fire WARN_ON().  Is
NULL definitely not a supported input to icc_put() per its contract?

--- [PATCH 56/61] clk ---

> -	if (!clk || WARN_ON_ONCE(IS_ERR(clk)))
> +	if (WARN_ON_ONCE(IS_ERR_OR_NULL(clk)))
>  		return;

This applies to both clk_unregister() and __clk_put().  The latter is
called by clk_put(), which is the put-side counterpart for
devm_clk_get_optional().  When an optional clock is absent, clk_get_optional()
returns NULL, and the corresponding clk_put(NULL) call goes through
__clk_put(NULL).  That path was a silent no-op before; it would now
WARN_ON_ONCE() for every optional-clock teardown.  clk_unregister(NULL)
looks more clearly wrong, but __clk_put(NULL) seems like a supported
calling convention.

--- [PATCH 57/61] reset ---

> -	if (!rstc || WARN_ON(IS_ERR(rstc)))
> +	if (WARN_ON(IS_ERR_OR_NULL(rstc)))
>  		return;

Same concern as icc_put(): optional reset controls obtained via
reset_control_get_optional() return NULL, and their corresponding
reset_control_release(NULL) teardown would now fire WARN_ON().

--- [PATCH 58/61] arch/x86 callthunks ---

> -	if (!dest || WARN_ON_ONCE(IS_ERR(dest)))
> +	if (WARN_ON_ONCE(IS_ERR_OR_NULL(dest)))
>  		return;

call_get_dest() can return NULL when the instruction at addr is not a
direct call (e.g., during early patching).  The original code treats NULL
as a normal, silent skip.  Turning that into WARN_ON_ONCE() would produce
spurious warnings on every non-call site visited by patch_call().  This
one looks most likely to fire in practice.

For the remaining patches in the series (1-54, 59-61) the conversions
appear semantically equivalent and the rhashtable walk patterns
(patches 10, 15, 16, 26, 29, 31, 38, 42) follow the established kernel
idiom correctly.

series.title: selftests/cgroup: skip test_zswap if zswap is globally disabled
series.author: Li Wang <liwang@redhat.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v3 1/7] selftests/cgroup: skip test_zswap if zswap is globally disabled
[PATCH v3 2/7] selftests/cgroup: avoid OOM in test_swapin_nozswap
[PATCH v3 3/7] selftests/cgroup: use runtime page size for zswpin check

[PATCH v3 5/7] selftests/cgroup: replace hardcoded page size values in test_zswap
[PATCH v3 6/7] selftest/cgroup: fix zswap test_no_invasive_cgroup_shrink on large pagesize system
[PATCH v3 7/7] selftest/cgroup: fix zswap attempt_writeback() on 64K pagesize system

review.summary: This series fixes zswap cgroup selftests to handle non-4K page sizes (arm64/ppc64 64K pages) and a runtime zswap-disabled state, replacing hardcoded constants with sysconf(_SC_PAGESIZE) and adding an enabled-state check in main(). The changes are largely correct but there are two small defects worth fixing.
review.review_inline:
Li Wang <liwang@redhat.com>

selftests/cgroup: skip test_zswap if zswap is globally disabled

[ patches 1-3 look fine, one nit in patch 2 ]

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_zswap.c b/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_zswap.c

[ patch 1/7 - check_zswap_enabled() ]

> +	char value[2];
> +
> +	if (access("/sys/module/zswap", F_OK))
> +		ksft_exit_skip("zswap isn't configured\n");
> +
> +	if (read_text("/sys/module/zswap/parameters/enabled", value,
> +						sizeof(value)) <= 0)
> +		ksft_exit_fail_msg("Failed to read "
> +				"/sys/module/zswap/parameters/enabled\n");
> +
> +	if (value[0] == 'N')
> +		ksft_exit_skip("zswap is disabled (hint: echo 1 > "
> +				"/sys/module/zswap/parameters/enabled)\n");

read_text() reads max_len-1 bytes and NUL-terminates, so with a 2-byte
buffer we get exactly one character ('Y' or 'N') in value[0] and '\0'
in value[1].  The check for 'N' is correct and the sizing is fine.

[ patch 2/7 - test_swapin_nozswap() ]

> +	long swap_peak, zswpout, min_swap;
> +	size_t control_allocation_size = sysconf(_SC_PAGESIZE) * 512;
> +
> +	min_swap = control_allocation_size / 4;
> +	snprintf(mem_max_buf, sizeof(mem_max_buf), "%zu", control_allocation_size * 3/4);

[ ... ]

> +	if (swap_peak < min_swap) {
> +		ksft_print_msg("at least %ldMB of memory should be swapped out\n",
> +				min_swap / MB(1));

On a 4K page size system, control_allocation_size is 4096*512 = 2MB,
so min_swap = 524288 bytes.  MB(1) expands to (1 << 20) = 1048576, and
min_swap / MB(1) = 0 by integer division.  The diagnostic would print
"at least 0MB of memory should be swapped out" even though the real
threshold is 512KB.  Could you print bytes directly, or at least use
KB units (min_swap >> 10) with a %ldKB format?

[ patches 3/7 and 4/7 look fine ]

[ patch 5/7 - global pagesize, attempt_writeback() ]

> +static size_t pagesize;

[ ... ]

> -	long pagesize = sysconf(_SC_PAGESIZE);
>  	size_t memsize = MB(4);
>  	char buf[pagesize];

Removing the local sysconf() call and relying on the global is safe
because main() sets pagesize before any cg_run() fork, and forked
children inherit the parent's memory.  The VLA char buf[pagesize] with
a size_t argument is valid.

[ patch 6/7 - test_no_invasive_cgroup_shrink() ]

> +	snprintf(zswap_max_buf, sizeof(zswap_max_buf), "%ld", pagesize);

pagesize is declared as static size_t, so the correct format specifier
is %zu, not %ld.  On ILP32 or LLP64 targets size_t and long can differ
in width, and this would produce a -Wformat compiler warning.  The
analogous mem_max_buf snprintf() in the same function correctly uses
%zu for control_allocation_size; please use %zu here as well.

[ patch 7/7 looks fine ]

series.title: mm/zswap, zsmalloc: Per-memcg-lruvec zswap accounting
series.author: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH 00/11] mm/zswap, zsmalloc: Per-memcg-lruvec zswap accounting
[PATCH 01/11] mm/zsmalloc: Rename zs_object_copy to zs_obj_copy
[PATCH 02/11] mm/zsmalloc: Make all obj_idx unsigned ints
[PATCH 03/11] mm/zsmalloc: Introduce conditional memcg awareness to zs_pool
[PATCH 04/11] mm/zsmalloc: Introduce objcgs pointer in struct zspage
[PATCH 05/11] mm/zsmalloc: Store obj_cgroup pointer in zspage
[PATCH 06/11] mm/zsmalloc, zswap: Redirect zswap_entry->objcg to zspage
[PATCH 07/11] mm/zsmalloc, zswap: Handle objcg charging and lifetime in zsmalloc
[PATCH 08/11] mm/memcontrol: Track MEMCG_ZSWAPPED in bytes
[PATCH 09/11] mm/vmstat, memcontrol: Track ZSWAP_B, ZSWAPPED_B per-memcg-lruvec
[PATCH 10/11] mm/zsmalloc: Handle single object charge migration in migrate_zspage
[PATCH 11/11] mm/zsmalloc: Handle charge migration in zpdesc substitution

review.summary: This series moves memcg accounting for zswap from the zswap layer into zsmalloc, adding per-zspage objcg arrays, per-lruvec NR_ZSWAP_B/NR_ZSWAPPED_B tracking at byte granularity, and migration handlers to update those counters when objects or zpdescs move between NUMA nodes. The core design is sound but patch 11 contains an accounting bug where the node-level compressed_stat counter (NR_ZSWAP_B) is not updated when a zpdesc migrates to a different NUMA node.
review.review_inline:
Author: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

mm/zsmalloc, zsmalloc: Per-memcg-lruvec zswap accounting

Overall the design is well-thought-out. A few issues below.

> --- [PATCH 09/11] mm/vmstat, memcontrol: Track ZSWAP_B, ZSWAPPED_B per-memcg-lruvec ---

[ ... ]

> diff --git a/mm/zsmalloc.c b/mm/zsmalloc.c
> @@ -823,6 +823,9 @@ static void __free_zspage(...)
> +		if (pool->memcg_aware)
> +			dec_node_page_state(zpdesc_page(zpdesc),
> +					    pool->compressed_stat);

and in alloc_zspage:

> +		if (pool->memcg_aware)
> +			inc_node_page_state(zpdesc_page(zpdesc),
> +					    pool->compressed_stat);

So the node-level compressed_stat (NR_ZSWAP_B) is incremented once per
allocated zpdesc (at alloc_zspage time) and decremented once per freed
zpdesc (at __free_zspage time). This means the node-level counter
tracks the physical page, not the object bytes. The comment in the
commit message that "NR_ZSWAP_B can be kept accurate" seems to be
referring to this per-page accounting. OK so far.

Note that mod_memcg_lruvec_state (as confirmed by reading
__mod_memcg_lruvec_state in mm/memcontrol.c) only updates the per-memcg
and per-lruvec percpu counters; it does NOT touch pgdat->vm_stat. So
the node-level NR_ZSWAP_B is exclusively maintained by the
inc/dec_node_page_state calls in alloc_zspage and __free_zspage.

> --- [PATCH 11/11] mm/zsmalloc: Handle charge migration in zpdesc substitution ---

> +static void zs_page_migrate_lruvec(struct zs_pool *pool, struct zspage *zspage,
> +				   struct zpdesc *old_zpdesc,
> +				   struct zpdesc *new_zpdesc,
> +				   struct size_class *class)
> +{
> +	int size = class->size;
> +	int old_nid = page_to_nid(zpdesc_page(old_zpdesc));
> +	int new_nid = page_to_nid(zpdesc_page(new_zpdesc));
> +	...
> +	if (old_nid == new_nid)
> +		return;

For cross-node zpdesc substitution the function correctly updates the
lruvec-level compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat via
mod_memcg_lruvec_state, and it updates the node-level uncompressed_stat
via dec/inc_node_page_state inside the object loop. But there is no
corresponding dec/inc_node_page_state for the node-level
compressed_stat (NR_ZSWAP_B).

The node-level NR_ZSWAP_B was incremented by 1 for old_zpdesc at
alloc_zspage time (attributed to old_nid). After replace_sub_page,
the zspage chain contains new_zpdesc, so when __free_zspage eventually
runs it will call dec_node_page_state on new_zpdesc's page (new_nid).
The result:
  old_nid: permanently +1 (never decremented for this page)
  new_nid: decremented at free without ever being incremented

For a pool with even modest migration activity across NUMA nodes this
will cause old_nid NR_ZSWAP_B to grow unboundedly and new_nid
NR_ZSWAP_B to go negative.

The fix belongs in zs_page_migrate_lruvec after the old_nid == new_nid
early-return:

  dec_node_page_state(zpdesc_page(old_zpdesc), pool->compressed_stat);
  inc_node_page_state(zpdesc_page(new_zpdesc), pool->compressed_stat);

> +	for (off = first_obj_offset;
> +			off < PAGE_SIZE && idx < class->objs_per_zspage;
> +			idx++, off += size) {
> +		struct obj_cgroup *objcg = zspage->objcgs[idx];
> +		int bytes_on_page = min_t(int, size, PAGE_SIZE - off);
> +
> +		if (!objcg)
> +			continue;
> +
> +		zs_migrate_lruvec(pool, objcg, old_nid, new_nid,
> +				  bytes_on_page, size);
> +
> +		dec_node_page_state(zpdesc_page(old_zpdesc),
> +				    pool->uncompressed_stat);
> +		inc_node_page_state(zpdesc_page(new_zpdesc),
> +				    pool->uncompressed_stat);
> +	}

The uncompressed_stat handling looks correct: only objects that start
on old_zpdesc (with an objcg) contribute 1 to the node-level
NR_ZSWAPPED_B, and that 1 is moved to new_zpdesc's node. Objects
without an objcg were never charged, so skipping them is right.

The boundary spanning-object case handled before this loop passes
bytes_on_page=first_obj_offset to zs_migrate_lruvec, which only
updates lruvec-level stats (no node-level dec/inc for that object,
because its node-level NR_ZSWAPPED_B was charged on the previous
zpdesc where it started). That is also correct.

> --- [PATCH 06/11] mm/zsmalloc, zswap: Redirect zswap_entry->objcg to zspage ---

> +	objcg = zs_lookup_objcg(entry->pool->zs_pool, entry->handle);
> +	if (objcg)
> +		count_objcg_events(objcg, ZSWPWB, 1);
>  
>  	zswap_entry_free(entry);

In zswap_writeback_entry the objcg is looked up via zs_lookup_objcg
(acquiring the zspage read lock) for count_objcg_events. Then
zswap_entry_free is called, which calls zs_lookup_objcg a second time
for zswap_lru_del. Two lock round-trips where one would be sufficient.
Not a correctness problem, just worth noting in case it shows up on
profiles.

> --- [PATCH 10/11] mm/zsmalloc: Handle single object charge migration in migrate_zspage ---

> +	dec_node_page_state(zpdesc_page(s_zpdesc), pool->uncompressed_stat);
> +	inc_node_page_state(zpdesc_page(d_zpdesc), pool->uncompressed_stat);

These dec/inc happen even when s_zpdesc and d_zpdesc are on the same
node. The net effect is zero but two unnecessary atomic updates are
issued. A guard of if (nids[0] != nids[2]) would avoid the noise.
Again not a correctness issue.
