review.summary: This series fixes mm selftests to work correctly on PowerPC with 64KB base pages, addressing hugetlb cgroup path construction, tmpfs sizing, mremap test sizing, and various skip/error handling improvements across 13 patches.
review.review_inline:
Sayali Patil <sayalip@linux.ibm.com>

selftests/mm: fix failures and robustness improvements

Good motivation overall -- the 64KB base page portability issues are
real and the fixes are generally correct.  A few things worth
looking at below.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/hugetlb_reparenting_test.sh b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/hugetlb_reparenting_test.sh

[ ... ]

> +  # Spawn write_to_hugetlbfs in a separate task to ensure correct cgroup accounting
> +  ./write_to_hugetlbfs -p "$path" -s "$size" -m 0 -o -d & pid=$!
> +  echo "$pid" > "$cg_file"
> +  wait "$pid"

The sleep(1) added via -d is processed by write_to_hugetlbfs during
getopt() argument parsing.  That ordering is correct -- the sleep
finishes before the switch(method) block runs -- so the parent has
well over a second to write the PID.  What is missing is any error
check on the echo.  The script has set -e so a write failure would
exit immediately, leaving the child running as an orphan.  On a
cgroup v2 system, writing to cgroup.procs can fail (e.g. no memory
controller enabled, per-cgroup permission) and the failure would
silently abandon the allocation under the wrong cgroup without a
clear diagnostic.  Is there a reason not to check the return of the
echo and emit an explicit error message before aborting?

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/write_to_hugetlbfs.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/write_to_hugetlbfs.c

[ ... ]

> +		case 'd':
> +			sleep(1);
> +			break;

The -d option is last in the command line used by the caller
(-p ... -s ... -m 0 -o -d), so sleep(1) fires at the end of option
parsing, before any allocation.  That ordering makes the 1-second
window work.  However, if someone later adds -d earlier in the call
or calls write_to_hugetlbfs with -d before -o, the sleep would fire
before populate is set and the parent might write the PID after the
child is already done.  A comment explaining that -d must come after
all other options, or making the delay unconditional (e.g. a named
pipe for synchronization), would prevent future confusion.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/run_vmtests.sh b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/run_vmtests.sh

[ ... ]

> +		# Ensure hwpoison_inject is available for memory-failure tests
> +		if [ "${CATEGORY}" = "memory-failure" ]; then
> +			# Try to load hwpoison_inject if not present.
> +			HWPOISON_DIR=/sys/kernel/debug/hwpoison/
> +			if [ ! -d "$HWPOISON_DIR" ]; then
> +				if ! modprobe -n hwpoison_inject > /dev/null 2>&1; then
> +					echo "Module hwpoison_inject not found, skipping..." \
> +						| tap_prefix
> +					skip=1
> +				else
> +					modprobe hwpoison_inject > /dev/null 2>&1
> +					LOADED_MOD=1
> +				fi
> +			fi

[ ... ]

> +		# Unload hwpoison_inject if we loaded it
> +		if [ -n "${LOADED_MOD}" ]; then
> +			modprobe -r hwpoison_inject > /dev/null 2>&1
> +		fi

LOADED_MOD is assigned inside run_test() but is never declared
local and never unset after use.  After the memory-failure call
completes, LOADED_MOD=1 persists in the environment.  If any future
patch adds a run_test() call after the memory-failure block, that
test will unconditionally attempt modprobe -r hwpoison_inject
(silently) before running.  This is a latent bug -- it should either
be declared local at the top of run_test() or explicitly unset after
the modprobe -r call.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/hugepage-mremap.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/hugepage-mremap.c

[ ... ]

> -#define FLAGS (MAP_SHARED | MAP_ANONYMOUS)
> +#define FLAGS (MAP_HUGETLB | MAP_SHARED | MAP_POPULATE)

[ ... ]

> +	void *vaddr = mmap((void *)suggested_addr, length, PROTECTION, FLAGS, fd, 0);

Now haddr, daddr, and vaddr all map the same fd at offset 0 with
MAP_POPULATE.  Three MAP_POPULATE mappings of the same file-backed
hugetlb fd at offset 0 will pre-fault the same physical huge pages
three times.  For a shared file-backed mapping the physical pages
are de-duplicated in the page cache, so the three faults should
just increment reference counts rather than allocate three separate
sets of huge pages.  The existing nr_hugepages setup in
run_vmtests.sh allocates 256MB worth of pages; with a 1GB huge page
and length=2GB (from patch 6), this would require at least 2 huge
pages.  Is the hugepage pool guaranteed to have enough pages to
satisfy MAP_POPULATE for all three mmaps?  With the previous code,
vaddr was MAP_ANONYMOUS so it did not consume huge pages at mmap
time.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_hugetlb_memcg.c b/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_hugetlb_memcg.c

[ ... ]

> +	if (nr_pages < MEMCG_CHARGE_BATCH && current == old_current) {
> +		/*
> +		 * Memory cgroup charging uses per-CPU stocks and batched updates to the
> +		 *  memcg usage counters. For hugetlb allocations, the number of pages
> +		 *  that memcg charges is expressed in base pages (nr_pages), not
> +		 *  in hugepage units. When the charge for an allocation is smaller than
> +		 *  the internal batching threshold  (nr_pages <  MEMCG_CHARGE_BATCH),
> +		 *  it may be fully satisfied from the CPU's local stock. In such
> +		 *  cases memory.current does not necessarily
> +		 *  increase.
> +		 *  Therefore, Treat a zero delta as valid behaviour here.
> +		 */
> +		ksft_print_msg("no visible memcg charge, allocation consumed from local stock.\n");
> +	} else if (!values_close(expected_current, current, 5)) {

The condition `current == old_current` only accepts an exact zero
delta as the "charged from stock" case.  If the per-CPU stock was
nearly full, the charge could be partially flushed, making current
somewhere between old_current and old_current + hpage_size -- not
equal to old_current, but also not within 5% of expected_current.
In that scenario the else-if triggers and the test fails spuriously.
Would it be safer to accept any current < expected_current when
nr_pages < MEMCG_CHARGE_BATCH, rather than requiring exactly
current == old_current?

review.review_inline:
Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>

mm: Fix demotion gfp by preserving initial gfp reclaim policy

Patches 1 and 2 look like clean mechanical moves with no functional
change.  Patch 3 is correct: GFP_TRANSHUGE expands to
GFP_TRANSHUGE_LIGHT | __GFP_DIRECT_RECLAIM, so the old ordering
silently re-enabled direct reclaim after clearing it.  Swapping the
order is the right fix.

One question on patch 4:

> diff --git a/mm/migrate.c b/mm/migrate.c
> --- a/mm/migrate.c
> +++ b/mm/migrate.c

[ ... ]

> +	gfp_t gfp_mask, gfp_entry;

[ ... ]

> +	gfp_mask = gfp_entry = mtc->gfp_mask;

[ ... ]

> 	if (folio_test_hugetlb(src)) {
> 		struct hstate *h = folio_hstate(src);
> 
> 		gfp_mask = htlb_modify_alloc_mask(h, gfp_mask);
> +		gfp_mask = (gfp_mask & ~__GFP_RECLAIM) | (gfp_entry & __GFP_RECLAIM);

htlb_modify_alloc_mask() rebuilds the mask from htlb_alloc_mask(h),
which returns __GFP_COMP | __GFP_NOWARN | GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE.  Of
the incoming gfp_mask, it preserves only __GFP_THISNODE and
__GFP_NOWARN -- everything else in gfp_entry is silently dropped.

The fix correctly restores __GFP_RECLAIM from gfp_entry, but the
demotion gfp_mask also carries __GFP_NOMEMALLOC (to forbid use of
emergency reserves).  After htlb_modify_alloc_mask() that flag is
gone, and this line does not restore it.  Should the expression also
preserve __GFP_NOMEMALLOC from gfp_entry, i.e.:

    gfp_mask = (gfp_mask & ~(__GFP_RECLAIM | __GFP_NOMEMALLOC))
             | (gfp_entry & (__GFP_RECLAIM | __GFP_NOMEMALLOC));

or is the loss of __GFP_NOMEMALLOC for hugetlb demotion intentional?
The commit that introduced the hugetlb path (19fc7bed252c) has the
same issue, so this may be pre-existing, but the same fix site seems
like the natural place to address it if it matters.

review.review_inline:
Breno Leitao <leitao@debian.org>

kho: kexec-metadata: track previous kernel chain

Patches 1-4 look good to me.  Comments on patch 5 below.

> diff --git a/kernel/liveupdate/kexec_handover.c b/kernel/liveupdate/kexec_handover.c

[ ... ]

> +static void __init kho_in_kexec_metadata(void)
> +{
> +	struct kho_kexec_metadata *metadata;
> +	phys_addr_t metadata_phys;
> +	int err;
> +
> +	err = kho_retrieve_subtree(KHO_METADATA_NODE_NAME, &metadata_phys,
> +				   NULL);
> +	if (err)
> +		/* This is fine, previous kernel didn't export metadata */
> +		return;
> +	metadata = phys_to_virt(metadata_phys);
> +
> +	/*
> +	 * Copy data to the kernel structure that will persist during
> +	 * kernel lifetime.
> +	 */
> +	kho_in.kexec_count = metadata->kexec_count;
> +	strscpy(kho_in.previous_release, metadata->previous_release,
> +		sizeof(kho_in.previous_release));
> +
> +	pr_info("exec from: %s (count %u)\n", kho_in.previous_release,
> +					      kho_in.kexec_count);
> +}

After copying both fields from metadata into kho_in, the preserved page
from the old kernel is no longer needed.  Nothing calls
kho_restore_free(metadata) here, so the page allocated by the previous
kernel's kho_out_kexec_metadata() stays reserved in memblock and is
never returned to the page allocator.  That is a one-page leak per
kexec boot.  On systems that kexec frequently this accumulates.

Should kho_restore_free(metadata) be called after the strscpy(), once
the data has been safely copied into kho_in?

[ ... ]

> +static int __init kho_kexec_metadata_init(const void *fdt)
> +{
> +	int err;
> +
> +	if (fdt)
> +		kho_in_kexec_metadata();
> +
> +	/* Populate kexec metadata for the possible next kexec */
> +	err = kho_out_kexec_metadata();
> +	if (err)
> +		pr_warn("failed to initialize kexec-metadata subtree: %d\n",
> +			err);
> +
> +	return err;
> +}

The pr_warn signals that this failure is non-critical, but the returned
error code propagates into kho_init() and triggers goto err_free_fdt,
which sets kho_enable = false and shuts down all of KHO.  A transient
allocation failure in an optional debugging subtree would disable LUO,
memblock preservation, and every other KHO consumer for the lifetime of
this boot.

Should kho_kexec_metadata_init() return 0 unconditionally (keeping only
the pr_warn), so a metadata setup failure does not take down the entire
KHO subsystem?

> +static __init int kho_init(void)
> +{
> ...
> +	err = kho_kexec_metadata_init(fdt);
> +	if (err)
> +		goto err_free_fdt;

This is the call site that becomes fatal if kho_out_kexec_metadata()
fails.  It may be worth confirming the desired behaviour: should a
failed metadata subtree abort all of KHO initialisation, or should KHO
continue and simply omit the metadata on the next kexec?

Several concrete bugs were found in the minidump driver and the printk/sparse registration code.
review.review_inline:
Eugen Hristev <eugen.hristev@linaro.org>
Mukesh Ojha <mukesh.ojha@oss.qualcomm.com>

[PATCH v2 00/25] Introduce meminspect

Thanks for the series. I have a few concerns that need to be addressed
before this can be merged.

> diff --git a/kernel/printk/printk.c b/kernel/printk/printk.c
> --- a/kernel/printk/printk.c
> +++ b/kernel/printk/printk.c

[ ... ]

> 	local_irq_restore(flags);
> 
> +	meminspect_register_va(new_log_buf, new_log_buf_len);
> +	meminspect_register_va(new_descs, new_descs_size);
> +	meminspect_register_va(new_infos, new_infos_size);

The registrations land after local_irq_restore(), but new_log_buf_len
is a static __initdata that was already zeroed to 0 inside the
irq-save/restore region ("new_log_buf_len = 0" is done just before
"prb = &printk_rb_dynamic"). So meminspect_register_va() receives
new_log_buf with size 0, registering nothing useful. The correct size
for the new log buffer at this point is log_buf_len, which was set from
new_log_buf_len just before it was zeroed. Should this use log_buf_len
instead?

> diff --git a/mm/sparse.c b/mm/sparse.c
> --- a/mm/sparse.c
> +++ b/mm/sparse.c

[ ... ]

> again:
> 	usage = memblock_alloc_try_nid(size, SMP_CACHE_BYTES, goal, limit, nid);
> 	if (!usage && limit) {
> 		limit = MEMBLOCK_ALLOC_ACCESSIBLE;
> 		goto again;
> 	}
> +
> +	meminspect_register_va(usage, size);
> +
>  	return usage;

If the second allocation attempt (after the goto-again with
limit = MEMBLOCK_ALLOC_ACCESSIBLE) also returns NULL, the code falls
through and calls meminspect_register_va(NULL, size). The caller handles
the NULL return from this function, but does meminspect_register_va
handle a NULL address gracefully? A guard like "if (usage)" before the
registration would make the intent explicit and avoid any question.

> diff --git a/drivers/soc/qcom/minidump.c b/drivers/soc/qcom/minidump.c
> new file mode 100644
> --- /dev/null
> +++ b/drivers/soc/qcom/minidump.c

[ ... ]

> +static int qcom_md_get_region_index(struct minidump *md, int id)
> +{
> +	unsigned int count = le32_to_cpu(md->toc->region_count);
> +	unsigned int i;
> +
> +	for (i = 0; i < count; i++)
> +		if (md->regions[i].seq_num == id)
> +			return i;
> +
> +	return -ENOENT;
> +}

The function returns a signed int (with -ENOENT on failure), but in
unregister_md_region below:

> +	unsigned int idx;
> +
> +	idx = qcom_md_get_region_index(md, e->id);
> +	if (idx < 0) {
> +		dev_dbg(md->dev, "%d region is not present\n", e->id);
> +		return;
> +	}

idx is declared as unsigned int. When qcom_md_get_region_index()
returns -ENOENT, the negative value is converted to a very large
unsigned integer. The subsequent "if (idx < 0)" comparison against an
unsigned is always false, so the not-found case is never caught. The
code then falls through to:

> +	memmove(&mdr[idx], &mdr[idx + 1],
> +		(region_cnt - idx - 1) * sizeof(*mdr));

With idx holding UINT_MAX or similar, this memmove will read and write
far outside the regions array, corrupting arbitrary kernel memory.
idx should be declared as int.

Additionally, in qcom_md_get_region_index(), the comparison
"md->regions[i].seq_num == id" compares a __le32 field against a plain
int without le32_to_cpu(). Likewise in register_md_region():

> +	mdr->seq_num = e->id;

This assigns to a __le32 field without cpu_to_le32(). On little-endian
platforms (all current Qualcomm targets) this happens to work, but it
is incorrect for any big-endian build and will generate sparse warnings.
Should this use cpu_to_le32(e->id) and le32_to_cpu() on the read side?

series.title: lib/maple_tree: fix potential NULL dereference in mas_pop_node()
series.author: Josh Law <hlcj1234567@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v2] lib/maple_tree: fix potential NULL dereference in mas_pop_node()

review.summary: Adds a NULL check after kmem_cache_alloc_from_sheaf() in mas_pop_node() to prevent a memset NULL dereference, but the premise is incorrect: the sheaf API guarantees successful allocation up to the prefilled count, and even if the check were valid, all callers dereference the returned pointer without a NULL check, so the crash would only move downstream.
review.review_inline:
Author: Josh Law <hlcj1234567@gmail.com>

lib/maple_tree: fix potential NULL dereference in mas_pop_node()

Thank you for the patch.  After tracing the sheaf allocation
contracts and all callers of mas_pop_node(), I have two concerns
that I think need to be addressed before this can be applied.

> If kmem_cache_alloc_from_sheaf() returns NULL (possible under
> GFP_NOWAIT pressure), mas_pop_node() falls through to the out label
> and dereferences the NULL pointer in memset(ret, 0, sizeof(*ret)).

The commit message's premise does not match the documented contract
of the sheaf API.  The comment above
kmem_cache_alloc_from_sheaf_noprof() in mm/slub.c reads:

  "Guaranteed not to fail as many allocations as was the requested
   size.  After the sheaf is emptied, it fails - no fallback to the
   slab cache itself."

The sheaf is a pre-filled object array.  It does not perform any new
allocation when objects are popped from it; GFP_NOWAIT pressure is
therefore irrelevant at the pop site.  mas_alloc_nodes() pre-fills
the sheaf with exactly node_request nodes under the caller's chosen
gfp flags and signals -ENOMEM via mas_set_err() if that pre-fill
fails.  Callers of mas_alloc_nodes() check for that error before
proceeding to call mas_pop_node(), so when mas_pop_node() is reached
the sheaf is guaranteed to have a node available.

The only scenario where kmem_cache_alloc_from_sheaf() returns NULL
in mas_pop_node() is if mas_pop_node() is called more times than
mas_alloc_nodes() was asked to prepare for -- an accounting bug.
If such a bug exists, the right fix is to correct the accounting,
not to add a NULL check here.

> diff --git a/lib/maple_tree.c b/lib/maple_tree.c
> --- a/lib/maple_tree.c
> +++ b/lib/maple_tree.c

[ ... ]

>  	ret = kmem_cache_alloc_from_sheaf(maple_node_cache, GFP_NOWAIT, mas->sheaf);
> +	if (!ret)
> +		return NULL;
>  
>  out:
>  	memset(ret, 0, sizeof(*ret));

Even setting aside the premise, does this return NULL actually help?
All of the call sites I can find dereference the result immediately
without any NULL check.  For example, the path through
mas_wr_node_store():

    node = mas_pop_node(mas);
    pivots = ma_pivots(node, type);   /* crash here if node is NULL */

and the path through mas_dup_copy():

    cp->dst[i].node = ma_mnode_ptr(mas_pop_node(mas));
    /* cp->dst[i].node is used later without a NULL check */

Returning NULL moves the crash from memset() to the first use site,
but does not prevent a crash.  If mas_pop_node() is supposed to be
able to return NULL in a recoverable way, each caller would need
corresponding error handling, which is a much larger change.

The existing WARN_ON_ONCE(!mas->sheaf) already returns NULL for the
"impossible" programmer-error case, suggesting that returning NULL
from mas_pop_node() has never been treated as a recoverable
condition by the callers.

Could you clarify:
1. Is there a concrete reproducer or call path showing sheaf->size
   reaches zero inside mas_pop_node() today?
2. If yes, is the fix an accounting correction upstream, or is full
   NULL-return propagation through all callers the intended approach?

series.title: mm/damon: Support hot application detections
series.author: <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>

patches.title: [RFC PATCH v2 0/4] mm/damon: Support hot application detections
[RFC PATCH v2 1/4] mm/damon: Generic context creation for modules
[RFC PATCH v2 2/4] mm/damon: Support for synchrounous huge pages collapse
[RFC PATCH v2 3/4] mm/damon: New module with hot application detection
[RFC PATCH v2 4/4] DAMON_HOT_HUGEPAGE documentation

review.summary: This series introduces a new DAMON-based module that collapses hot virtual memory regions into huge pages, identified by monitoring user-supplied PIDs. It adds a new DAMOS_COLLAPSE action mapping to MADV_COLLAPSE and refactors the existing module context constructor to accept an ops mode parameter.
review.review_inline:
From: Asier Gutierrez <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>
Subject: [RFC PATCH v2 0/4] mm/damon: Support hot application detections

Thanks for the series.  There are a few things that need attention
before this can merge.

--- [RFC PATCH v2 1/4] mm/damon: Generic context creation for modules ---

> diff --git a/mm/damon/modules-common.c b/mm/damon/modules-common.c
> -int damon_modules_new_paddr_ctx_target(struct damon_ctx **ctxp,
> -		struct damon_target **targetp)
> +int damon_modules_new_ctx_target(struct damon_ctx **ctxp,
> +		struct damon_target **targetp, enum damon_ops_id mode)

The refactoring is clean.  All existing callers are updated to pass
DAMON_OPS_PADDR so behaviour is preserved.

--- [RFC PATCH v2 2/4] mm/damon: Support for synchronous huge pages collapse ---

> diff --git a/include/linux/damon.h b/include/linux/damon.h
> +	DAMOS_COLLAPSE,
>  	DAMOS_STAT,		/* Do nothing but only record the stat */
>  	NR_DAMOS_ACTIONS,

Inserting DAMOS_COLLAPSE between DAMOS_MIGRATE_COLD and DAMOS_STAT
shifts the integer value of DAMOS_STAT from 9 to 10 and changes
NR_DAMOS_ACTIONS from 10 to 11.  The sysfs interface uses string names
for scheme actions, so existing sysfs users are safe.  But anything
that serialises or hard-codes the integer values of these enumerators
(BPF programs, user-space tools reading /proc, out-of-tree modules)
would silently get the wrong action.  The safer placement is after
DAMOS_STAT, immediately before NR_DAMOS_ACTIONS.

> diff --git a/mm/damon/vaddr.c b/mm/damon/vaddr.c
> +	case DAMOS_COLLAPSE:
> +		madv_action = MADV_COLLAPSE;
> +		break;

The case is placed between DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE and DAMOS_MIGRATE_HOT.
MADV_COLLAPSE performs a synchronous THP collapse and can sleep, which
is fine here since damon_va_apply_scheme is called from the kdamond
thread.  Looks correct.

--- [RFC PATCH v2 3/4] mm/damon: New module with hot application detection ---

> +++ b/mm/damon/hugepage.c

[ ... ]

> +struct mutex enable_disable_lock;

Two problems here.  First, this is missing the static keyword, which
exports the symbol into the global namespace and risks colliding with
other modules.  Second, the proper declaration for a global mutex is
DEFINE_MUTEX(enable_disable_lock), not a bare struct declaration.
A zero-initialized mutex may happen to work today but is not a
guaranteed or lockdep-clean initialisation.

[ ... ]

> +static int damon_hugepage_apply_parameters(
> +				struct hugepage_task *monitored_task,
> +				int min_access,
> +				enum damos_action action)
> +{
> +	struct damos *scheme;
> +	struct damon_ctx *param_ctx;
> +	struct damon_target *param_target;
> +	struct damos_filter *filter;
> +	int err;
> +	struct pid *spid;
> +
> +	err = damon_modules_new_ctx_target(&param_ctx, &param_target,
> +					   DAMON_OPS_VADDR);
> +	if (err)
> +		return err;
> +
> +	spid = find_get_pid(monitored_task->pid);
> +	if (!spid)
> +		return err;

This is a double bug.  At the point where find_get_pid() returns NULL,
err is 0 because the preceding damon_modules_new_ctx_target() call
succeeded.  The function returns 0, telling the caller that everything
is fine, when it actually failed to locate the target process.

More critically, param_ctx (with param_target already added to it) is
not freed.  This leaks both the context and the target on every call
where the monitored PID has already exited.  The fix needs both a
negative error code and a jump to the out label:

    if (!spid) {
        err = -ESRCH;
        goto out;
    }

> +	err = damon_set_attrs(param_ctx, &damon_hugepage_mon_attrs);
> +	if (err)
> +		goto out;
> +
> +	err = -ENOMEM;
> +	scheme = damon_hugepage_new_scheme(min_access, action);
> +	if (!scheme)
> +		goto out;
> +
> +	damon_set_schemes(param_ctx, &scheme, 1);
> +
> +	filter = damos_new_filter(DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ANON, true, false);
> +	if (!filter)
> +		goto out;
> +	damos_add_filter(scheme, filter);
> +
> +	err = damon_commit_ctx(monitored_task->ctx, param_ctx);
> +out:
> +	damon_destroy_ctx(param_ctx);
> +	return err;

After damon_set_schemes() the scheme is owned by param_ctx, so the
damon_destroy_ctx() in the out path handles cleanup for the filter-
allocation failure case.  That part looks correct.

[ ... ]

> +static struct damos_watermarks damon_hugepage_wmarks = {
> +	.metric = DAMOS_WMARK_FREE_MEM_RATE,
> +	.interval = 5000000, /* 5 seconds */
> +	.high = 900, /* 90 percent */
> +	.mid = 800, /* 80 percent */
> +	.low = 50, /* 5 percent */
> +};

With high=900 the module is inactive only when free memory exceeds 90%,
which means it is almost always active.  More importantly these
watermark defaults make little semantic sense for a hugepage collapsing
workload: THP collapsing should not depend on memory pressure at all.
DAMON_RECLAIM uses watermarks to avoid reclaiming when memory is
plentiful; that logic does not apply here.  Please explain the intended
activation policy, or consider using DAMOS_WMARK_NONE if the module
should always be active.

--- [RFC PATCH v2 4/4] DAMON_HOT_HUGEPAGE documentation ---

> +How It Works?
> +=============
> +
> +DAMON_HOT_HUGEPAGE spawns a new kthread which will monitor the applications in
> +the system. The monitor thread will calculate the moving average of the sum of
> +utimes of all the threads for all the processes. Then, pick the top three and
> +launch a damon process to monitor the hot regions in those tasks.
> +
> +If a task turns cold, the monitor thread will detect it (it will not fall in the
> +top three hot tasks), and stop the damon thread for that task.

This entire section describes the automatic hot-application detection
mechanism that the cover letter says was removed in v2 based on
SeongJae Park's feedback.  The documentation still refers to a
supervisor thread, moving averages, utime ranking, and the "top three"
selection, none of which exist in the v2 code.  The "How It Works"
section needs to be rewritten to describe what the module actually does:
accept a user-supplied list of PIDs via monitored_pids, launch one
kdamond per PID, and adaptively lower the min_access threshold until
collapses occur.

> +    # cd /sys/module/damon_dynamic_hotpages/parameters/
> +    # echo 3811,10123,12111 > monitored_pids

The module parameter prefix in hugepage.c is "damon_hugepage." and the
module name would be "hugepage", but the documentation example uses
"damon_dynamic_hotpages".  These need to be consistent.

series.title: lib/idr: Fixes for infinite loop and memory leak
series.author: Josh Law <hlcj1234567@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v3 0/2] lib/idr: Fixes for infinite loop and memory leak
[PATCH v3 1/2] lib/idr: fix infinite loop in idr_get_next()
[PATCH v3 2/2] lib/idr: fix memory leak in ida_alloc_range() error path

review.summary: Two patches for lib/idr: patch 1 sets *nextid = INT_MAX in idr_get_next() when id > INT_MAX is detected, and patch 2 claims to fix a memory leak in ida_alloc_range() but only adds a test with no corresponding lib/idr.c change.
review.review_inline:
Josh Law <hlcj1234567@gmail.com>

lib/idr: Fixes for infinite loop and memory leak

Reviewed against idr.h and lib/idr.c from mm-new.

--- Patch 1/2: lib/idr: fix infinite loop in idr_get_next() ---

> diff --git a/lib/idr.c b/lib/idr.c
> --- a/lib/idr.c
> +++ b/lib/idr.c

[ ... ]

> -	if (WARN_ON_ONCE(id > INT_MAX))
> +	if (WARN_ON_ONCE(id > INT_MAX)) {
> +		*nextid = INT_MAX;
>  		return NULL;
> +	}

The motivating scenario needs clarification.  Looking at the
idr_for_each_entry macro in idr.h:

    for (id = 0; ((entry) = idr_get_next(idr, &(id))) != NULL; id += 1U)

When idr_get_next() returns NULL (because it found an entry at > INT_MAX
but couldn't represent it), the for-loop condition evaluates to false and
the loop exits immediately.  The value of *nextid at that point is
irrelevant for this iteration pattern.

Is there a concrete in-tree caller that actually loops forever without
this fix?  If the motivation is the idr_for_each_entry_continue() macro,
that also terminates on NULL entry.  A pointer to a reproducer or a
specific call chain that actually spins would help justify the change.

The fix itself is still a reasonable improvement to the API output
contract -- if NULL is returned due to an out-of-range id, setting
*nextid to INT_MAX makes it clear the caller has reached the end of the
valid int range.  But the commit message should not describe an infinite
loop that has not been demonstrated.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/radix-tree/idr-test.c b/tools/testing/...
> --- a/tools/testing/radix-tree/idr-test.c
> +++ b/tools/testing/radix-tree/idr-test.c

[ ... ]

>  		BUG_ON(ptr != NULL);
> -		BUG_ON(sid != 0);
> +		BUG_ON(sid != INT_MAX);

This update is correct given the new behavior.

--- Patch 2/2: lib/idr: fix memory leak in ida_alloc_range() error path ---

The commit message says:

    "Reorder the error handling to ensure `alloc` is properly freed
    when an XArray error occurs."

But the diff for this patch only modifies idr-test.c.  There is no
corresponding change to lib/idr.c.  The alleged memory leak is present
in the current source: in the xa_is_value branch of ida_alloc_range(),
when kzalloc_obj() succeeds (storing the result in the local `bitmap`
variable, not `alloc`), and the subsequent xas_store() fails, the code
jumps to `out` where:

    if (bitmap != alloc)
        kfree(alloc);  /* alloc is NULL, this is a no-op */

The allocated bitmap is lost.  The same pattern exists in the else
branch when bit >= BITS_PER_XA_VALUE and alloc is NULL.  If this fix
was intentionally split from the test, where is the lib/idr.c patch?

> diff --git a/tools/testing/radix-tree/idr-test.c b/tools/testing/...
> --- a/tools/testing/radix-tree/idr-test.c
> +++ b/tools/testing/radix-tree/idr-test.c

[ ... ]

> +	ida_check_leak();
>  	ida_alloc_free_test();

ida_check_leak() is called here inside user_ida_checks(), but the
function definition appears later in the file.  C requires a declaration
before use; this will fail to compile without a forward declaration.
All other functions called from user_ida_checks() (ida_check_nomem,
ida_check_conv_user, ida_check_random, ida_alloc_free_test) are defined
before user_ida_checks().

> +static void ida_check_leak(void)
> +{
> +	DEFINE_IDA(ida);
> +
> +	/* Allocate up to 128 to ensure we need a new bitmap */
> +	ida_alloc_range(&ida, 0, 128, GFP_KERNEL);
> +	
> +	/* Force a failure by providing an invalid range */
> +	ida_alloc_range(&ida, 0, 0, GFP_KERNEL);
> +	
> +	ida_destroy(&ida);
> +}

This test does not exercise the code path described in the commit
message.  After ida_alloc_range(&ida, 0, 128, GFP_KERNEL) allocates one
ID (say, ID 0), the second call ida_alloc_range(&ida, 0, 0, GFP_KERNEL)
asks for an ID in [0, 0].  ID 0 is already occupied, so xas_find_marked
finds no free slot in that range and the function falls through to the
nospc label, which already calls kfree(alloc).  No xas_store() failure
occurs and the leaked-bitmap path is never reached.

To actually hit the xas_store() failure in the xa_is_value branch, the
XArray must need to expand (allocate a new tree node) while under
GFP_NOWAIT, fail that allocation, and land in xas_error.  That path
requires controlled memory pressure, not just a range collision.  The
test as written does not cover the claimed bug.

There is also trailing whitespace on the blank lines inside
ida_check_leak().

series.title: mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
series.author: Leno Hou via B4 Relay <devnull+lenohou.gmail.com@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 0/2] mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
[PATCH v2 1/2] mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
[PATCH v2 2/2] mm/mglru: maintain workingset refault context across state transitions

review.summary: This series introduces a 'draining' intermediate state (lru_drain_core static branch) to prevent OOM kills during MGLRU enable/disable transitions, and separately encodes the originating reclaim mode in shadow entries to maintain workingset refault correctness across state changes.
review.review_inline:
From: Leno Hou <lenohou@gmail.com>

mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
mm/mglru: maintain workingset refault context across state transitions

The general approach of using a draining state to bridge the reclaim
vacuum is sound.  A few issues need addressing before this is ready.

> diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c

[ ... ]

> +DEFINE_STATIC_KEY_FALSE(lru_drain_core);
> +static inline bool lru_gen_draining(void)
> +{
> +	return static_branch_unlikely(&lru_drain_core);
> +}

This is defined as a file-scoped symbol but is referenced from
multiple sites within vmscan.c.  However, lru_drain_core has no extern
declaration and is not exported; this is fine as long as all uses stay
in vmscan.c.  Confirmed.

[ ... ]

> @@ -5178,6 +5190,8 @@ static void lru_gen_change_state(bool enabled)
>  	if (enabled == lru_gen_enabled())
>  		goto unlock;
>  
> +	static_branch_enable_cpuslocked(&lru_drain_core);
> +
>  	if (enabled)
>  		static_branch_enable_cpuslocked(&lru_gen_caps[LRU_GEN_CORE]);
>  	else

The lru_drain_core enable happens before the LRU_GEN_CORE state flip.
During the window between those two operations lru_gen_enabled() is
still true, so a concurrent reclaimer in shrink_lruvec sees
(true || true) and falls through to the traditional LRU scan after
lru_gen_shrink_lruvec().  The traditional LRU lists are still empty at
that point, so get_scan_count() returns zeroes and the scan loop is
a no-op.  This is harmless but worth a comment explaining the ordering
intent.

> @@ -5780,9 +5797,12 @@ static void shrink_lruvec(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct scan_control *sc)
> -	if (lru_gen_enabled() && !root_reclaim(sc)) {
> +	if ((lru_gen_enabled() || lru_gen_draining()) && !root_reclaim(sc)) {
>  		lru_gen_shrink_lruvec(lruvec, sc);
> -		return;
> +
> +		if (!lru_gen_draining())
> +			return;
> +
>  	}

When disabling MGLRU, lru_gen_draining() is true and
lru_gen_enabled() is false.  lru_gen_shrink_lruvec() is called even
though MGLRU is globally disabled.  This is safe because
try_to_shrink_lruvec() calls get_nr_to_scan() which reads
lrugen->nr_pages[] under READ_ONCE(); once drain_evictable() has
cleared those counters (under lruvec_lock), get_nr_to_scan() returns
0 and try_to_shrink_lruvec() exits immediately.  This path is safe
but needs a comment, since calling lru_gen_shrink_lruvec() with
lru_gen_enabled()==false is non-obvious.

The second lru_gen_draining() check after lru_gen_shrink_lruvec()
has a TOCTOU window: draining might have just finished between the
two checks.  If lru_gen_draining() races to false here, the function
returns without scanning traditional LRU lists.  At that instant the
migration is complete, so pages are fully on one side; the next
reclaim invocation will find them.  This is benign, but the comment
should acknowledge it.

> @@ -6041,11 +6061,17 @@ static void shrink_node(pg_data_t *pgdat, struct scan_control *sc)
>  	un
> [... truncated ...]

The shrink_node hunk is cut off in the submitted diff.  Please resubmit
with the complete patch; the shrink_node and kswapd_age_node changes
are mentioned in the commit log as part of the fix but cannot be
evaluated here.

---

> diff --git a/mm/workingset.c b/mm/workingset.c

[ ... ]

> +#define WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT  1
>  #define WORKINGSET_SHIFT 1
>  #define EVICTION_SHIFT	((BITS_PER_LONG - BITS_PER_XA_VALUE) +	\
> +			 WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT + \
>  			 WORKINGSET_SHIFT + NODES_SHIFT + \
>  			 MEM_CGROUP_ID_SHIFT)

EVICTION_MASK is derived from EVICTION_SHIFT.  Consuming one more bit
from the eviction timestamp narrows the refault window.  On a system
with few memcgs, few NUMA nodes, and a large address space this may
be acceptable, but it should be called out explicitly in the commit
message with the new effective precision.

> @@ -214,6 +217,7 @@ static void unpack_shadow(void *shadow, int *memcgidp, pg_data_t **pgdat,
> +	entry >>= WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT;
>  	workingset = entry & ((1UL << WORKINGSET_SHIFT) - 1);
>  	entry >>= WORKINGSET_SHIFT;

unpack_shadow() silently discards the is_mglru bit.  Callers that need
it must extract it from xa_to_value(shadow) before calling
unpack_shadow().  Both workingset_test_recent() and
workingset_refault() do this correctly, but a comment here explaining
why the bit is being dropped (and that callers are responsible for
pre-extracting it) would prevent future callers from getting this
wrong silently.

> @@ -430,8 +430,10 @@ bool workingset_test_recent(void *shadow, bool file, bool *workingset,
> +	unsigned long entry = xa_to_value(shadow);
> +	bool is_mglru = !!(entry & WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT);

WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT is defined as 1 (a shift count / field width),
but here it is used as a bitmask.  This works today only because the
value of the macro happens to equal BIT(0).  In workingset_refault()
the same bit is tested as:

    entry & ((1UL << WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT) - 1)

which is the correct derivation of a mask from a shift constant and
would scale correctly if WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT were ever changed.
Please use that form consistently, or rename the macro to
WORKINGSET_MGLRU_MASK and define it as BIT(0) directly.

> @@ -545,11 +545,11 @@ void workingset_refault(struct folio *folio, void *shadow)
> +	unsigned long entry = xa_to_value(shadow);
>  
>  	VM_BUG_ON_FOLIO(!folio_test_locked(folio), folio);
>  
> -	if (lru_gen_enabled()) {
> +	if (entry & ((1UL << WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT) - 1)) {
>  		lru_gen_refault(folio, shadow);
>  		return;
>  	}

This correctly dispatches based on the shadow entry's origin rather
than the current global state.  One concern: lru_gen_refault() calls
lru_gen_test_recent() which calls unpack_shadow() internally.
unpack_shadow() now shifts away the is_mglru bit before extracting
workingset.  For a shadow produced by lru_gen_eviction() (is_mglru=1
at bit 0, workingset at bit 1), unpack_shadow() correctly shifts bit 1
into position 0 before extracting workingset.  This is consistent.

However, shadow entries that were created by an older kernel (without
the is_mglru bit) will be misinterpreted: the old workingset bit at
bit 0 becomes the new is_mglru bit, and the old node ID bits shift
into the workingset position.  Shadow entries are short-lived so in
practice this only affects the brief kexec-free upgrade window, but
it is worth acknowledging in the commit message.

series.title: tools/testing/vma: add unit tests for vma_flags_[empty, diff_pair, and]()
series.author: "Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle)" <ljs@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH 02/20] tools/testing/vma: add unit tests for vma_flags_[empty, diff_pair, and]()

review.summary: Adds unit tests for vma_flags_empty(), vma_flags_diff_pair(), vma_flags_and_mask(), and vma_flags_and(), along with test-only vma_flags_same_mask()/vma_flags_same() helpers and corresponding ASSERT_FLAGS_* macros. Test logic is sound and edge cases are handled correctly via NUM_VMA_FLAG_BITS guards.
review.review_inline:
Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>

tools/testing/vma: add unit tests for vma_flags_[empty, diff_pair, and]()

Test logic looks correct throughout.  One small inconsistency in the
new helper:

> diff --git a/tools/testing/vma/include/custom.h b/tools/testing/vma/include/custom.h
> --- a/tools/testing/vma/include/custom.h
> +++ b/tools/testing/vma/include/custom.h

[ ... ]

> +/* Place here until needed in the kernel code. */
> +static __always_inline bool vma_flags_same_mask(vma_flags_t *flags,
> +						vma_flags_t flags_other)
> +{
> +	const unsigned long *bitmap = flags->__vma_flags;
> +	const unsigned long *bitmap_other = flags_other.__vma_flags;
> +
> +	return bitmap_equal(bitmap, bitmap_other, NUM_VMA_FLAG_BITS);
> +}

The first parameter is a non-const pointer, but the function only reads
from it (note how bitmap is declared const inside the body).  The rest
of the read-only mask helpers -- vma_flags_test_any_mask(),
vma_flags_test_all_mask() -- both take `const vma_flags_t *flags`.
Should this be `const vma_flags_t *flags` to stay consistent with that
convention, and to make it easier to promote to kernel code later?

series.title: mm/vma: convert vm_flags_t to vma_flags_t in vma code
series.author: "Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle)" <ljs@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH 00/20] mm/vma: convert vm_flags_t to vma_flags_t in vma code
[PATCH 01/20] mm/vma: add vma_flags_empty(), vma_flags_and(), vma_flags_diff_pair()
[PATCH 02/20] tools/testing/vma: add unit tests flag empty, diff_pair, and[_mask]

[PATCH 05/20] mm/vma: use new VMA flags for sticky flags logic

review.summary: This 20-patch series introduces a bitmap-based vma_flags_t type and progressively converts VMA flag operations in mm/vma.c, mmap(), mprotect(), mlock(), madvise(), mseal(), and userfaultfd from the legacy vm_flags_t scalar to the new type, adding a comprehensive set of flag helper functions and macro wrappers along the way.
review.review_inline:
Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>

mm/vma: convert vm_flags_t to vma_flags_t in vma code

The conversion looks mechanically correct overall.  A couple of
points worth discussing below.

--- [PATCH 11/20] mm/vma: introduce [vma_flags,legacy]_to_[legacy,vma_flags]() helpers ---

> diff --git a/include/linux/mm_types.h b/include/linux/mm_types.h
> +static __always_inline vma_flags_t legacy_to_vma_flags(vm_flags_t flags)
> +{
> +	vma_flags_t ret;
> +
> +	ret.__vma_flags[0] = (unsigned long)flags;
> +	return ret;
> +}

The local variable ret is declared without initialisation before
only __vma_flags[0] is set.  Today this is harmless because
NUM_VMA_FLAG_BITS == BITS_PER_LONG so the bitmap has exactly one
word, and that word is written before the struct is returned.

However mk_vma_flags() and __mk_vma_flags() both start from
EMPTY_VMA_FLAGS, giving a clear zero-then-set pattern.  If
NUM_VMA_FLAG_BITS were ever widened before this helper is removed,
the upper words would contain stack garbage and any caller that
tested those bits (e.g. via vma_flags_empty() or a diff-pair
check) would see false positives.

Would it be worth initialising ret from EMPTY_VMA_FLAGS here for
consistency with the rest of the flag-building helpers?

--- [PATCH 19/20] mm/vma: convert vma_modify_flags[_uffd]() to use vma_flags_t ---

> diff --git a/mm/mlock.c b/mm/mlock.c
> @@ ... @@
> +static void mlock_vma_pages_range(struct vm_area_struct *vma,
> +	unsigned long start, unsigned long end,
> +	vma_flags_t *new_vma_flags)

The original prototype passed newflags by value; the new one passes
a pointer and mutates it (setting then clearing VMA_IO_BIT).  The
caller (mlock_fixup) does not read new_vma_flags after the call
returns, so the mutation is harmless.  But it is a subtle change in
calling convention.  Is there a reason to prefer the pointer over
a by-value copy as the original had?

> +	if (vma_flags_test(new_vma_flags, VMA_LOCKED_BIT))
> +		vma_flags_set(new_vma_flags, VMA_IO_BIT);
> +	vma_start_write(vma);
> +	WRITE_ONCE(vma->flags, *new_vma_flags);

The original vm_flags_reset_once() did WRITE_ONCE only on
__vma_flags[0].  The new WRITE_ONCE(vma->flags, *new_vma_flags)
operates on the full struct.  With NUM_VMA_FLAG_BITS == BITS_PER_LONG
the struct is exactly one machine word so compiletime_assert_rwonce_type
passes and the generated code is identical.  But the comment in
vm_flags_reset_once() explicitly noted "write once behaviour is
required only for the first system word" precisely to handle a future
wider bitmap.  If this function outlives the transitional helpers,
that assumption will silently break.  Worth a comment?

> @@ ... @@ static int mlock_fixup(...)
> +	vma_flags_t new_vma_flags = legacy_to_vma_flags(newflags);
> +	const vma_flags_t old_vma_flags = vma->flags;
>  	struct mm_struct *mm = vma->vm_mm;
>  	int nr_pages;
>  	int ret = 0;
> -	vm_flags_t oldflags = vma->vm_flags;
> -
> -
>

Two extra blank lines are introduced between the variable
declarations and the condition below.  Style nit only.

series.title: guest_memfd: Track amount of memory allocated on inode
series.author: Ackerley Tng <ackerleytng@google.com>

patches.title: [PATCH RFC v3 0/4] guest_memfd: Track amount of memory allocated on inode
[PATCH RFC v3 1/4] KVM: guest_memfd: Track amount of memory allocated on inode
[PATCH RFC v3 2/4] KVM: guest_memfd: Set release always on guest_memfd mappings
[PATCH RFC v3 3/4] KVM: selftests: Wrap fstat() to assert success
[PATCH RFC v3 4/4] KVM: selftests: Test that st_blocks is updated on allocation

review.summary: This series makes guest_memfd track allocated bytes in the inode's i_blocks/i_bytes fields by calling inode_add_bytes() at folio creation time in kvm_gmem_get_folio() and inode_sub_bytes() in a new .invalidate_folio() callback, enabled via mapping_set_release_always().
review.review_inline:
Author: Ackerley Tng <ackerleytng@google.com>

KVM: guest_memfd: Track amount of memory allocated on inode

Two accounting correctness concerns below.

> diff --git a/virt/kvm/guest_memfd.c b/virt/kvm/guest_memfd.c

[ ... ]

> -	folio = __filemap_get_folio_mpol(inode->i_mapping, index,
> -					 FGP_LOCK | FGP_ACCESSED | FGP_CREAT,
> -					 mapping_gfp_mask(inode->i_mapping), policy);
> -	mpol_cond_put(policy);
> +	folio = __filemap_get_folio_mpol(inode->i_mapping, index,
> +					 FGP_LOCK | FGP_ACCESSED | FGP_CREAT,
> +					 mapping_gfp_mask(inode->i_mapping), policy);
> +	mpol_cond_put(policy);
> +
> +	if (!IS_ERR(folio))
> +		inode_add_bytes(inode, folio_size(folio));

This placement has a TOCTOU race. kvm_gmem_get_folio() first tries
kvm_gmem_get_folio_noalloc() (fast path). If that misses, it falls
through to __filemap_get_folio_mpol() with FGP_CREAT. These two
lookups are not atomic.

Consider two threads both calling kvm_gmem_get_folio() for the
same index concurrently:

  Thread A: kvm_gmem_get_folio_noalloc() -> IS_ERR (miss)
  Thread B: kvm_gmem_get_folio_noalloc() -> IS_ERR (miss)
  Thread A: __filemap_get_folio_mpol() creates folio, calls
            inode_add_bytes()
  Thread B: __filemap_get_folio_mpol() finds the folio A just
            inserted (either via filemap_get_entry() or via the
            -EEXIST -> goto repeat path) and also calls
            inode_add_bytes()

Both threads call inode_add_bytes() for the same folio, doubling
the count. kvm_gmem_get_pfn() and the vm_fault handler can both
call kvm_gmem_get_folio() concurrently for the same index, so
this race is reachable.

Is there a way to detect that the slow-path returned an existing
folio rather than a newly created one? One option would be to
check folio_test_uptodate() on return, since newly allocated
folios are not marked uptodate until kvm_gmem_prepare_folio().
Another option is to move the accounting into the folio creation
path more tightly, e.g. using a folio flag set under i_lock when
the folio is first inserted.

[ ... ]

> +static void kvm_gmem_invalidate_folio(struct folio *folio, size_t offset, size_t len)
> +{
> +	size_t bytes = folio_size(folio);
> +
> +	WARN_ON_ONCE(offset);
> +	WARN_ON_ONCE(len != bytes);
> +
> +	inode_sub_bytes(folio_inode(folio), bytes);
> +}

The kvm_gmem_uffd_ops path (kvm_gmem_filemap_add) inserts folios
into the mapping directly via filemap_add_folio(), bypassing
kvm_gmem_get_folio() entirely, so inode_add_bytes() is never called
for those folios.

If a uffd-added folio is subsequently removed by kvm_gmem_punch_hole()
via truncate_inode_pages_range() -> truncate_cleanup_folio() ->
folio_invalidate() -> kvm_gmem_invalidate_folio(), inode_sub_bytes()
is called without a prior inode_add_bytes(), causing the byte count
to underflow.

The mmap fault path (kvm_gmem_fault_user_mapping) does go through
kvm_gmem_get_folio() and would get accounted. But the uffd path
uses a separate alloc+filemap_add sequence that skips the accounting.
Should kvm_gmem_filemap_add() call inode_add_bytes() too?

> diff --git a/virt/kvm/guest_memfd.c b/virt/kvm/guest_memfd.c
> --- a/virt/kvm/guest_memfd.c
> +++ b/virt/kvm/guest_memfd.c
> @@ -607,6 +607,7 @@ static int __kvm_gmem_create(struct kvm *kvm, loff_t size, u64 flags)
>  	mapping_set_inaccessible(inode->i_mapping);
>  	/* Unmovable mappings are supposed to be marked unevictable as well. */
>  	WARN_ON_ONCE(!mapping_unevictable(inode->i_mapping));
> +	mapping_set_release_always(inode->i_mapping);

The AS_RELEASE_ALWAYS flag is documented as "Call ->release_folio(),
even if no private data", but guest_memfd has no ->release_folio op.
The actual purpose here is to make folio_needs_release() return true
so that truncate_cleanup_folio() calls folio_invalidate(), which
triggers the ->invalidate_folio() accounting callback.

This works correctly because both paths gate on folio_needs_release(),
but a comment explaining the indirect coupling would help future
readers understand why this flag is set.

series.title: ACPI: Add support for ACPI RAS2 feature table
series.author: <shiju.jose@huawei.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v17 0/2] ACPI: Add support for ACPI RAS2 feature table
[PATCH v17 1/2] ACPI:RAS2: Add driver for the ACPI RAS2 feature table
[PATCH v17 2/2] ras: mem: Add ACPI RAS2 memory driver

review.summary: The patch adds an ACPI RAS2 feature table driver with PCC-based communication and an auxiliary memory scrubbing driver that integrates with the EDAC subsystem, including a new enable_demand sysfs attribute. Two concrete bugs found in ras2.c: a MMIO accessor size mismatch for the set_caps_status field and a type truncation in the MRTT delay path.
review.review_inline:
From: Shiju Jose <shiju.jose@huawei.com>

ACPI:RAS2: Add driver for the ACPI RAS2 feature table

> diff --git a/drivers/acpi/ras2.c b/drivers/acpi/ras2.c
> new file mode 100644
> --- /dev/null
> +++ b/drivers/acpi/ras2.c

[ ... ]

> +static int check_pcc_chan(struct ras2_sspcc *sspcc)
> +{
> +	struct acpi_ras2_shmem __iomem *gen_comm_base = sspcc->comm_addr;
> +	u32 cap_status;
> +	u16 status;
> +	int rc;

[ ... ]

> +	cap_status = readw_relaxed(&gen_comm_base->set_caps_status);

The set_caps_status field in struct acpi_ras2_shmem is declared as u32
in include/acpi/actbl2.h, but readw_relaxed() performs a 16-bit read.
This should be readl_relaxed().  On little-endian systems this happens
to return the correct value for the currently defined status codes
(0-6), but the upper two bytes of the field are silently discarded.
If firmware ever uses those upper bytes (reserved today, but a future
ACPI revision may define them), the driver will misinterpret the
status.

> +	writew_relaxed(0x0, &gen_comm_base->set_caps_status);

Same problem here: writew_relaxed() clears only the lower 16 bits of
the 32-bit set_caps_status field, leaving the upper 16 bits at
whatever the firmware wrote.  This should be writel_relaxed(0, ...).

[ ... ]

> +int ras2_send_pcc_cmd(struct ras2_mem_ctx *ras2_ctx, u16 cmd)
> +{

[ ... ]

> +	unsigned int time_delta;

[ ... ]

> +		time_delta = ktime_us_delta(ktime_get(), sspcc->last_cmd_cmpl_time);
> +		if (sspcc->pcc_mrtt > time_delta)
> +			udelay(sspcc->pcc_mrtt - time_delta);

ktime_us_delta() returns s64, but time_delta is unsigned int.  If
last_cmd_cmpl_time is zero-initialised (first call, no prior command),
ktime_us_delta() returns the full time-since-boot in microseconds.
After roughly 4295 seconds of uptime that value overflows unsigned int,
yielding a small wrapped result.  The comparison sspcc->pcc_mrtt >
time_delta could then become true and insert an unintended udelay.
time_delta should be s64 to match the return type of ktime_us_delta().

series.title: RDMA: Enable operation with DMA debug enabled
series.author: Leon Romanovsky <leon@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 0/8] RDMA: Enable operation with DMA debug enabled
[PATCH v2 1/8] dma-debug: Allow multiple invocations of overlapping entries
[PATCH v2 2/8] dma-mapping: handle DMA_ATTR_CPU_CACHE_CLEAN in trace output
[PATCH v2 3/8] dma-mapping: Clarify valid conditions for CPU cache line overlap
[PATCH v2 4/8] dma-mapping: Introduce DMA require coherency attribute
[PATCH v2 5/8] dma-direct: prevent SWIOTLB path when DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT is set
[PATCH v2 6/8] iommu/dma: add support for DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT attribute
[PATCH v2 7/8] RDMA/umem: Tell DMA mapping that UMEM requires coherency
[PATCH v2 8/8] mm/hmm: Indicate that HMM requires DMA coherency

review.summary: This series introduces DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT to enforce coherent-only DMA mappings (no SWIOTLB, no cache sync), fixes the spurious DMA debug overlap warning for cache-clean repeated mappings, renames DMA_ATTR_CPU_CACHE_CLEAN to DMA_ATTR_DEBUGGING_IGNORE_CACHELINES, and applies the new attribute to RDMA umem and HMM paths.
review.review_inline:
Author: Leon Romanovsky <leonro@nvidia.com>

RDMA: Enable operation with DMA debug enabled

> diff --git a/kernel/dma/debug.c b/kernel/dma/debug.c
> --- a/kernel/dma/debug.c
> +++ b/kernel/dma/debug.c

[ ... patch 3 hunk ... ]

> -	entry->is_cache_clean = !!(attrs & DMA_ATTR_CPU_CACHE_CLEAN);
> +	entry->is_cache_clean = attrs & DMA_ATTR_DEBUGGING_IGNORE_CACHELINES;

The field is declared as `bool is_cache_clean` in struct dma_debug_entry.
Removing the `!!` leaves an implicit unsigned-long-to-bool conversion.
In C the result is still correct (any non-zero value becomes true), but
the original `!!` was there to make the intent explicit. Patch 4 repeats
this pattern with the OR expression:

> +	entry->is_cache_clean = attrs & (DMA_ATTR_DEBUGGING_IGNORE_CACHELINES |
> +					 DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT);

The combined bitmask is (1UL<<11)|(1UL<<12) = 6144, which truncates to
true correctly, but is it intentional to drop `!!` here?  Restoring it
would keep the assignment self-documenting.

> diff --git a/kernel/dma/direct.h b/kernel/dma/direct.h
> --- a/kernel/dma/direct.h
> +++ b/kernel/dma/direct.h

[ ... ]

> -	if (attrs & DMA_ATTR_MMIO)
> +	if (attrs & (DMA_ATTR_MMIO | DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT))
>  		/* nothing to do: uncached and no swiotlb */
>  		return;

The comment "uncached and no swiotlb" only describes the MMIO case.
For DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT the buffer is coherent (not uncached),
so the comment is misleading.  Could you update it to cover both
reasons, e.g. "MMIO is uncached; REQUIRE_COHERENT skips sync and
swiotlb by construction"?

> diff --git a/kernel/dma/mapping.c b/kernel/dma/mapping.c
> --- a/kernel/dma/mapping.c
> +++ b/kernel/dma/mapping.c

[ ... ]

> +	if (!dev_is_dma_coherent(dev) && (attrs & DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT))
> +		return -EOPNOTSUPP;
>  
>  	if (WARN_ON_ONCE(!dev->dma_mask))
>  		return 0;

The new check in __dma_map_sg_attrs is placed before the dma_mask
sanity check.  If dev->dma_mask is NULL and the device happens to
be non-coherent with DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT set, the caller
receives -EOPNOTSUPP rather than triggering the WARN.  Is there a
reason the coherency check must precede the dma_mask check?  Placing
it after would preserve the existing WARN path for the NULL mask case.

> diff --git a/drivers/infiniband/core/umem.c b/drivers/infiniband/core/umem.c
> --- a/drivers/infiniband/core/umem.c
> +++ b/drivers/infiniband/core/umem.c

[ ... ]

>  	if (dirty)
>  		ib_dma_unmap_sgtable_attrs(dev, &umem->sgt_append.sgt,
> -					   DMA_BIDIRECTIONAL, 0);
> +					   DMA_BIDIRECTIONAL,
> +					   DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT);

ib_umem_get() can set dma_attr = DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT |
DMA_ATTR_WEAK_ORDERING when IB_ACCESS_RELAXED_ORDERING is requested.
__ib_umem_release() unmaps with DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT only.
DMA_ATTR_WEAK_ORDERING is not inspected during unmap in any current
path, so there is no functional regression, but the asymmetry between
map and unmap attrs is worth a comment explaining that DMA_ATTR_WEAK_ORDERING
is not needed at unmap time.

series.title: Fix race condition in the memory management system
series.author: Hubert Mazur <hmazur@google.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v1 0/1] Fix race condition in the memory management system
[PATCH v1 1/1] mm: fix race condition in the memory management

review.summary: The patch avoids a race by inserting fresh allocations directly into busy_areas and returning the pointer, skipping the free_areas round-trip. However it drops the mandatory set_memory_rox() and execmem_fill_trapping_insns() calls and tracks only the requested size rather than the full alloc_size in the maple tree, causing a memory leak.
review.review_inline:
Author: Hubert Mazur <hmazur@google.com>

mm: fix race condition in the memory management

The approach of bypassing free_areas for fresh allocations is sound,
but the implementation has two problems that need to be addressed.

> diff --git a/mm/execmem.c b/mm/execmem.c
> --- a/mm/execmem.c
> +++ b/mm/execmem.c

[ ... ]

> -static int execmem_cache_populate(struct execmem_range *range, size_t size)
> +static void *__execmem_cache_alloc(struct execmem_range *range, size_t size)
>  {
> +	struct maple_tree *busy_areas = &execmem_cache.busy_areas;
>  	unsigned long vm_flags = VM_ALLOW_HUGE_VMAP;
>  	struct vm_struct *vm;
> +	unsigned long addr;
>  	size_t alloc_size;
>  	int err = -ENOMEM;
>  	void *p;

[ ... ]

>  	vm = find_vm_area(p);
>  	if (!vm)
>  		goto err_free_mem;
>  
> +	/* Set new allocation as an already busy fragment */
> +	addr = (unsigned long)p;
> +	MA_STATE(mas, busy_areas, addr - 1, addr + 1);
> +	mas_set_range(&mas, addr, addr+size - 1);

The original execmem_cache_populate() called execmem_fill_trapping_insns()
and set_memory_rox() between find_vm_area() and the tree insertion.  Both
are missing here.

execmem_fill_trapping_insns() ensures unwritten bytes in the allocation are
filled with trap instructions.  set_memory_rox() transitions the pages from
PAGE_KERNEL to ROX, which is the whole point of CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_EXECMEM_ROX.
Without set_memory_rox(), memory returned through execmem_alloc() will be
PAGE_KERNEL (writable, NX) rather than ROX.  Callers that use execmem_alloc()
directly, without going through execmem_alloc_rw(), receive memory that is
not protected as the subsystem intends.

Could you add execmem_fill_trapping_insns(p, alloc_size) and
set_memory_rox((unsigned long)p, vm->nr_pages) here, before the
mas_store_gfp() call, mirroring what the original populate function did?

> +	mas_set_range(&mas, addr, addr+size - 1);

The vmalloc call above allocated alloc_size bytes, where alloc_size is
round_up(size, PMD_SIZE) (or size if the PMD-aligned allocation failed).
Only size bytes are recorded in the range here, so when this block later
passes through execmem_cache_free() -> __execmem_cache_free(), mas_range_len()
will return size and only those bytes are moved to free_areas.  The tail
bytes [addr+size, addr+alloc_size) are never tracked and cannot be
reclaimed by execmem_cache_clean().  That is a vmalloc memory leak whenever
alloc_size > size.

Should this use alloc_size rather than size here?  The consumer of the
range can still carve out what it needs (size bytes) at lookup time, just
as the old __execmem_cache_lookup() did from free_areas, but the full span
needs to be tracked somewhere so it can eventually be freed.

series.title: mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series.author: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

patches.title: [PATCH] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru

review.summary: Replaces the ad-hoc per-node/per-memcg deferred split queue with a proper list_lru, fixing the imperfect node-intersection problem in cgroup reclaim. The approach is architecturally sound but raises several questions about how the new caller-side locking API handles dying/reparented memcgs — a correctness invariant that the old split_queue_lock explicitly maintained via a retry-to-parent loop.
review.review_inline:
Author: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru

The overall direction is very welcome — list_lru is the right
infrastructure here, and replacing the bespoke per-memcg/per-node
queue with it cleans up a lot of code.  A few questions follow.

> diff --git a/include/linux/list_lru.h b/include/linux/list_lru.h

[ ... ]

> +/**
> + * list_lru_lock: lock the sublist for the given node and memcg
> + * @lru: the lru pointer
> + * @nid: the node id of the sublist to lock.
> + * @memcg: the cgroup of the sublist to lock.
> + *
> + * Returns the locked list_lru_one sublist. The caller must call
> + * list_lru_unlock() when done.
> + *
> + * You must ensure that the memcg is not freed during this call (e.g., with
> + * rcu or by taking a css refcnt).
> + *
> + * Return: the locked list_lru_one, or NULL on failure
> + */
> +struct list_lru_one *list_lru_lock(struct list_lru *lru, int nid,
> +				   struct mem_cgroup *memcg);
> +
> +/**
> + * list_lru_unlock: unlock a sublist locked by list_lru_lock()
> + * @l: the list_lru_one to unlock
> + */
> +void list_lru_unlock(struct list_lru_one *l);

Two related questions here.

First: the docstring says list_lru_lock() can return NULL, but
list_lru_unlock() takes a bare pointer with no null check.  Calling
list_lru_unlock(NULL) would be a crash.  Could the documentation
explicitly state that list_lru_unlock() must not be called when
list_lru_lock() returns NULL?

Second, and more importantly: looking at lock_list_lru_of_memcg() in
list_lru.c, there are two paths depending on the skip_empty argument.
With skip_empty=true it returns NULL when the sublist is dead or
missing; with skip_empty=false it follows the parent chain when
css_is_dying() is true.  The old folio_split_queue_lock() and
split_queue_lock() always followed the parent via an explicit retry
loop, so they never returned NULL.

The new deferred_split_folio(), __folio_unqueue_deferred_split(), and
split_huge_page_to_list_to_order() all need to replace those old
callers.  In particular, split_huge_page_to_list_to_order() acquires
the lock BEFORE folio_ref_freeze() precisely to prevent
deferred_split_scan() from racing with the split.  If
list_lru_lock_irqsave() returns NULL for a folio whose memcg has been
reparented (XArray entry cleared, items moved to parent), the
folio_ref_freeze() call cannot be safely made under any lock, and the
race window reopens.

Similarly for __folio_unqueue_deferred_split(): after reparenting, the
folio's _deferred_list is in the parent's list_lru_one, but
folio_memcg() still points to the dying child.  If list_lru_lock_irqsave()
returns NULL for the dying child's slot, the folio cannot be unqueued
before it is freed, leaving a dangling _deferred_list pointer in the
parent's list that deferred_split_scan() will later walk.

Could you clarify whether list_lru_lock() is implemented with
skip_empty=false (follow-parent, never returns NULL for a live lru)
or skip_empty=true?  If it's skip_empty=true, how do the callers in
huge_memory.c handle the NULL case for the two scenarios above?

[ ... ]

> diff --git a/mm/huge_memory.c b/mm/huge_memory.c

[ ... ]

> +	struct list_lru deferred_split_lru;
> +	static struct shrinker *deferred_split_shrinker;

[ ... ]

> +	if (list_lru_init_memcg(&deferred_split_lru, deferred_split_shrinker)) {
> +		shrinker_free(deferred_split_shrinker);
> +		return -ENOMEM;
> +	}
> +
> +	deferred_split_shrinker->count_objects = deferred_split_count;
> +	deferred_split_shrinker->scan_objects = deferred_split_scan;
> +	shrinker_register(deferred_split_shrinker);

[ ... ]

> +		if (!huge_zero_folio_shrinker) {
> +			list_lru_destroy(&deferred_split_lru);

Is shrinker_free(deferred_split_shrinker) also called here?  At this
point deferred_split_shrinker has already been registered via
shrinker_register(), so shrinker_free() is needed to properly
unregister and free it before returning -ENOMEM.

Also, does thp_shrinker_exit() gain a matching
list_lru_destroy(&deferred_split_lru) call?  The error path through
err_khugepaged in hugepage_init() reaches thp_shrinker_exit() after
thp_shrinker_init() has succeeded, so deferred_split_lru will have
been initialized.  Without a corresponding destroy, the lru->node
array allocated in __list_lru_init() leaks.

[ ... ]

> diff --git a/mm/memory.c b/mm/memory.c
> --- a/mm/memory.c
> +++ b/mm/memory.c

[ ... ]

The patch description says memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio() is called at
anon faults, swapin faults, and khugepaged collapse.  What does the
fault path do when memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio() fails?  Two outcomes
seem possible:

  1. The fault returns VM_FAULT_OOM.  That would be a regression:
     previously a THP anon fault had no dependency on this allocation;
     now it does.

  2. The error is silently swallowed.  Then the folio's memcg has no
     list_lru slot, and the subsequent call to deferred_split_folio()
     (via map_anon_folio_pmd_pf() -> deferred_split_folio()) will fail
     to enqueue the folio.  A partially-mapped THP that is never
     enqueued is never split.

Could you describe which path the implementation takes?

series.title: locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
series.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 0/2] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
[PATCH v2 1/2] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()
[PATCH v2 2/2] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint

review.summary: This series adds a contended_release tracepoint to the sleepable lock slowpath unlock paths, firing on the holder side when releasing a contended lock, and refactors percpu_up_read()'s slowpath into __percpu_up_read() to avoid binary bloat from inlining the tracepoint. The locking correctness is unaffected, but two percpu-rwsem tracepoint placements have semantic inconsistencies compared to all other call sites.
review.review_inline:
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>

locking: Add contended_release tracepoint

The locking correctness is preserved throughout; the tracepoint
placements in mutex, rtmutex, rwsem, semaphore, and rwbase_rt all
look correct.  Two percpu-rwsem sites have tracepoint semantics
that diverge from every other call site in the series.

> diff --git a/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c b/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
> --- a/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
> +++ b/kernel/locking/percpu-rwsem.c
> @@ -263,6 +263,8 @@ void percpu_up_write(struct percpu_rw_semaphore *sem)
>  {
>  	rwsem_release(&sem->dep_map, _RET_IP_);
>  
> +	trace_contended_release(sem);
> +
>  	/*
>  	 * Signal the writer is done, no fast path yet.

This trace fires before atomic_set_release(&sem->block, 0) and
before __wake_up(&sem->waiters, ...), so it fires while the write
lock is still held and before any waiter presence is known.
__wake_up() is unconditional here, but it can find an empty
waitqueue and wake nobody.  Every other call site in this series
places the tracepoint inside a conditional that confirms at least
one waiter exists (e.g. the if (waiter) block in
__mutex_unlock_slowpath, the if (tmp & RWSEM_FLAG_WAITERS) block
in __up_write).  Should this be guarded by a check like
!waitqueue_empty(&sem->waiters) before the tracepoint, and moved
to after atomic_set_release() so it fires at actual release time?

> @@ -297,6 +299,7 @@ void __percpu_up_read(struct percpu_rw_semaphore *sem)
>  	 * writer.
>  	 */
>  	smp_mb(); /* B matches C */
> +	trace_contended_release(sem);
>  	/*
>  	 * In other words, if they see our decrement (presumably to
>  	 * aggregate zero, as that is the only time it matters) they

__percpu_up_read() is called from percpu_up_read() whenever
rcu_sync_is_idle(&sem->rss) is false.  rcu_sync_is_idle() remains
false for an entire RCU grace period after percpu_up_write() calls
rcu_sync_exit(&sem->rss), even after the writer has fully released
the lock.  During that window, readers that acquired the lock after
percpu_up_write() returned (sem->block == 0, so they succeeded in
__percpu_down_read_trylock()) still take the slow release path and
call __percpu_up_read().  In that case rcuwait_wake_up(&sem->writer)
is a no-op because no writer is blocked, but trace_contended_release()
still fires.  This is a spurious emission: no waiter is being woken
and the lock release is not actually contended.

Could the tracepoint be guarded by checking whether sem->writer
actually has a waiter, e.g. via rcuwait_active(&sem->writer), before
firing?  That would align __percpu_up_read() with the guarantee the
other call sites provide: the tracepoint fires only when at least
one blocked task is about to be woken.

series.title: selftests/mm: skip several tests when thp is not available
series.author: Chunyu Hu <chuhu@redhat.com>

patches.title: [PATCH 0/4] selftests/mm: skip several tests when thp is not available

review.review_inline:
Author: Chunyu Hu <chuhu@redhat.com>

selftests/mm: skip several tests when thp is not available

This is a useful series that fixes real false-positive failures on RT
kernels. The logic in each patch is sound and the thp_is_enabled()
semantics (checking for 'always' or 'madvise' mode) correctly reflects
what each test actually requires.

One inconsistency is worth addressing:

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c
> --- a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c
> +++ b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c

[ ... ]

> +	if (!thp_is_enabled())
> +		ksft_exit_skip("Transparent Hugepages not available\n");
> +
>  	ksft_print_header();

The skip check is placed before ksft_print_header(), so when THP is
unavailable the TAP "version 13" header line is never emitted.  The
"after" output in the commit message confirms this: there is no
"TAP version 13" line before the "1..0 # SKIP" line.

Compare with split_huge_page_test.c in patch 3, where the check is
correctly placed after ksft_print_header() so the TAP header is
always emitted.  Could the check in transhuge-stress.c be moved to
after the ksft_print_header() call, matching that pattern?

[ ... ]

series.title: Accelerate page migration with batch copying and hardware offload
series.author: Shivank Garg <shivankg@amd.com>

patches.title: [RFC PATCH v4 0/6] Accelerate page migration with batch copying and hardware offload
[RFC PATCH v4 1/6] mm: introduce folios_mc_copy() for batch folio copying
[RFC PATCH v4 2/6] mm/migrate: skip data copy for already-copied folios

[RFC PATCH v4 6/6] mm/migrate: adjust NR_MAX_BATCHED_MIGRATION for testing

series.title: record non-slab shrinkers for non-root memcgs when kmem is disabled
series.author: Haifeng Xu <haifeng.xu@shopee.com>

patches.title: [PATCH V3 0/4] record non-slab shrinkers for non-root memcgs when kmem is disabled
[PATCH V3 1/4] mm: shrinker: add one more parameter in shrinker_id()
[PATCH V3 2/4] mm: shrinker: move shrinker_id() code block below memcg_kmem_online()
[PATCH V3 3/4] mm: shrinker: optimize the allocation of shrinker_info when setting cgroup_memory_nokmem
[PATCH V3 4/4] mm: shrinker: remove unnecessary check in shrink_slab_memcg()

review.summary: This series optimizes shrinker_info allocation for non-root memcgs when cgroup_memory_nokmem is set: instead of allocating a full-width bitmap indexed by shrinker_idr, non-root memcgs get a compact bitmap indexed by a separate shrinker_nonslab_idr covering only SHRINKER_NONSLAB|SHRINKER_MEMCG_AWARE shrinkers, with reparenting translated between the two namespaces when the parent is root.
review.review_inline:
Haifeng Xu <haifeng.xu@shopee.com>

mm: shrinker: record non-slab shrinkers for non-root memcgs when kmem
is disabled

Overall the design is sound and the dual-IDR approach is implemented
consistently.  A couple of things worth discussing:

> diff --git a/mm/shrinker.c b/mm/shrinker.c

[ ... ]

> +static int shrinker_memcg_alloc(struct shrinker *shrinker)
>  {
>  	int id, ret = -ENOMEM;
> +	bool kmem_online;
>  
>  	if (mem_cgroup_disabled())
>  		return -ENOSYS;
>  
> +	kmem_online = memcg_kmem_online();
>  	mutex_lock(&shrinker_mutex);

The V3 changelog says it moved the shrinker_nonslab_nr_max and
shrinker_nr_max reads inside shrinker_mutex, but kmem_online is
still captured before the lock.  This value then controls which
IDRs are populated and whether expand_shrinker_info() covers all
memcgs or root only.

In practice the ordering in mem_cgroup_css_online() saves this:
memcg_online_kmem() enables memcg_kmem_online_key before
alloc_shrinker_info() acquires shrinker_mutex, so no non-root
memcg can end up with a small shrinker_info after kmem goes
online.  But a NONSLAB shrinker registering in that brief window
will allocate an unused nonslab_id and update
shrinker_nonslab_nr_max unnecessarily.  Should kmem_online be
read under the mutex, consistent with what V3 did for the max
variables?

> +	shrinker->nonslab_id = -1;
> +	/*
> +	 * If cgroup_memory_nokmem is set, record shrinkers with SHRINKER_NONSLAB
> +	 * because memcg slab shrink only call non-slab shrinkers.
> +	 */
> +	if (!kmem_online && shrinker->flags & SHRINKER_NONSLAB) {

[ ... ]

> +		if (nonslab_id >= shrinker_nonslab_nr_max) {
> +			/* expand shrinker info for non-root memcgs */
> +			if (expand_shrinker_info(nonslab_id, false, false)) {
> +				idr_remove(&shrinker_idr, id);
> +				idr_remove(&shrinker_nonslab_idr, nonslab_id);
> +				goto unlock;
> +			}
> +		}
> +		shrinker->nonslab_id = nonslab_id;
> +	}
>  	shrinker->id = id;

The original shrinker_memcg_alloc() had an early return of -ENOSYS
for non-NONSLAB MEMCG_AWARE shrinkers when mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled()
was set, causing shrinker_alloc() to fall through to the non_memcg
path and clear SHRINKER_MEMCG_AWARE.  That early return is gone here.

Now those shrinkers stay MEMCG_AWARE, get an id in shrinker_idr, and
expand only root_mem_cgroup's shrinker_info.  Their deferred work is
accounted via root_mem_cgroup's shrinker_info rather than the global
nr_deferred array (shrinker->nr_deferred is NULL for MEMCG_AWARE
shrinkers, and the xchg/add_nr_deferred paths go through
xchg_nr_deferred_memcg with sc->memcg=root_mem_cgroup in the global
shrink path, which is safe).  Could you add a comment explaining this
intentional behavioral change for non-NONSLAB shrinkers?

> @@ -370,33 +370,47 @@ void reparent_shrinker_deferred(struct mem_cgroup *memcg)

[ ... ]

> +				if (!memcg_kmem_online() &&  parent == root_mem_cgroup) {
                                                 ^^
Double space before parent.

> diff --git a/mm/shrinker.c b/mm/shrinker.c
> --- a/mm/shrinker.c
> +++ b/mm/shrinker.c
> @@ -639,11 +639,6 @@ static unsigned long shrink_slab_memcg(gfp_t gfp_mask, int nid,
>  			}
>  			rcu_read_unlock();
>  
> -			/* Call non-slab shrinkers even though kmem is disabled */
> -			if (!memcg_kmem_online() &&
> -			    !(shrinker->flags & SHRINKER_NONSLAB))
> -				continue;
> -
>  			ret = do_shrink_slab(&sc, shrinker, priority);

Removing this is correct: after patch 3 switches to idr_find() on
shrinker_nonslab_idr for the kmem-offline path, only NONSLAB shrinkers
can ever be returned, making the filter permanently dead.

series.title: mm: improve map count checks
series.author: "Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle)" <ljs@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH 0/3] mm: improve map count checks
[PATCH 1/3] mm/mremap: correct invalid map count check
[PATCH 2/3] mm: abstract reading sysctl_max_map_count, and READ_ONCE()
[PATCH 3/3] mm/mremap: check map count under mmap write lock and abstract

review.summary: This series corrects mremap()'s map count checks: reducing the required headroom from 4 to 2 mappings, introducing a READ_ONCE wrapper for sysctl_max_map_count, and moving the early map count check under the mmap write lock. The locking fix and threshold correction are sound, but the early check in patch 3 is applied too broadly.
review.review_inline:
Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>

mm: improve map count checks

> diff --git a/mm/mremap.c b/mm/mremap.c

[ patch 1/3 ... ]

> -	if (current->mm->map_count >= sysctl_max_map_count - 3)
> +	if (current->mm->map_count + 2 > sysctl_max_map_count)

The new threshold (2 headroom) matches the post-659ace584e7a world
correctly.  The old equivalent was 4 headroom, which is now proven
unnecessary since __split_vma() in the unmap path no longer checks
map_count itself.

> -	if ((current->mm->map_count + 2) >= sysctl_max_map_count - 3)
> +	if (current->mm->map_count + 4 > sysctl_max_map_count)

Likewise, the early check in check_mremap_params() accounting for 2
pre-move unmaps (+2) plus 2 for the move itself (+2) = 4 total is
correct for the MREMAP_FIXED / MREMAP_DONTUNMAP case.

[ patch 2/3: READ_ONCE wrapper ... ]

The READ_ONCE/get_sysctl_max_map_count() wrapper is straightforward
and correct.  Moving the declaration to mm/internal.h is a good
cleanup since no code outside mm/ needs the raw variable.

[ patch 3/3 ]

> +static bool __check_map_count_against_split(struct mm_struct *mm,
> +					    bool before_unmaps)
> +{
> +	const int sys_map_count = get_sysctl_max_map_count();
> +	int map_count = mm->map_count;
> +
> +	mmap_assert_write_locked(mm);

Good: mmap_assert_write_locked() enforces that map_count is accessed
only under the write lock, which is the right constraint.

> +	if (before_unmaps)
> +		map_count += 2;
> +
> +	return map_count + 2 <= sys_map_count;

The arithmetic is correct: before_unmaps=true yields map_count+4 <=
sys_map_count (2 pre-move unmap splits + 2 for the copy+source-split),
and before_unmaps=false yields map_count+2 <= sys_map_count (just the
copy+source-split).  The ASCII diagrams make the accounting clear.

> +/* Do we violate the map count limit if we split VMAs prior to early unmaps? */
> +static bool check_map_count_against_split_early(void)
> +{
> +	return __check_map_count_against_split(current->mm,
> +					       /*before_unmaps=*/true);
> +}

I have a concern about where this is called:

>  	if (mmap_write_lock_killable(mm))
>  		return -EINTR;
>  	vrm->mmap_locked = true;
> +
> +	if (!check_map_count_against_split_early()) {
> +		mmap_write_unlock(mm);
> +		return -ENOMEM;
> +	}
> +
>  	if (vrm_move_only(vrm)) {
>  		res = remap_move(vrm);
>  	} else {
>  		vrm->vma = vma_lookup(current->mm, vrm->addr);
>  		res = check_prep_vma(vrm);
>  		if (res)
>  			goto out;
>  		res = vrm_implies_new_addr(vrm) ? mremap_to(vrm) : mremap_at(vrm);
>  	}

check_map_count_against_split_early() (with before_unmaps=true) is now
applied to every mremap path, including those that reach mremap_at().
Before this patch the corresponding check in check_mremap_params() was
guarded by the `if (!vrm_implies_new_addr(vrm)) return 0;` early exit,
so it was skipped for plain MREMAP_MAYMOVE calls without MREMAP_FIXED
or MREMAP_DONTUNMAP.

Two cases that were not previously subject to this check are now
subjected to the stricter map_count+4 threshold:

1. mremap_at() MREMAP_SHRINK (in-place shrink, no MREMAP_FIXED): the
   operation calls do_munmap() on the tail, which at worst splits one
   VMA, for a net map_count increase of +1.  The existing check in
   vms_gather_munmap_vmas() handles this at the right moment without
   requiring any prior headroom beyond map_count < sys_map_count.  The
   new early check requires map_count+4 <= sys_map_count, which is
   overly conservative by 3 slots.

2. expand_vma() with MREMAP_MAYMOVE when the VMA cannot grow in-place:
   this falls through to vrm_set_new_addr() + move_vma().  move_vma()
   calls prep_move_vma() which checks map_count+2 <= sys_map_count,
   which is the correct bound.  There is no MREMAP_FIXED destination
   unmap here, so the +2 added by before_unmaps=true is unwarranted.
   A caller with map_count = sys_map_count-3 that previously succeeded
   (passing the map_count+2 check in prep_move_vma) will now fail the
   early check (map_count+4 > sys_map_count).

Could check_map_count_against_split_early() be conditioned on
vrm_implies_new_addr(vrm) (as the old code implicitly was) so the
new locking correctness is preserved without broadening the scope of
the ENOMEM threshold for non-FIXED/DONTUNMAP paths?  Or is the
added conservatism intentional?
