series.title: Avoid pagewalk hugepage-split race with VFIO DMA set
series.author: Max Boone <mboone@akamai.com>

patches.title: [RFC 0/1] Avoid pagewalk hugepage-split race with VFIO DMA set
[RFC 1/1] mm/pagewalk: don't split device-backed huge pfnmaps

review.summary: The patch avoids splitting device-backed huge pfnmap PMD/PUD entries in the generic page walker to prevent a race where VFIO's fault handler can reinstall a PUD between split_huge_pud() and the pud_none() check, causing walk_pmd_range() to descend into what is now a huge leaf entry.
review.review_inline:
Max Boone <mboone@akamai.com>
mm/pagewalk: don't split device-backed huge pfnmaps

The core race is real and the mechanism is correct: insert_pud() and
insert_pmd() explicitly call pud_mkspecial()/pmd_mkspecial() for pfn
(non-folio) insertions, so pud_special()/pmd_special() reliably
identifies VFIO pfnmap entries on x86 and arm64.  On architectures
without CONFIG_ARCH_SUPPORTS_PUD_PFNMAP, pud_special() returns false
but vmf_insert_pfn_pud() is also unavailable, so the race cannot occur;
the behavior is consistent.

A few things worth discussing:

> diff --git a/mm/pagewalk.c b/mm/pagewalk.c

[ ... ]

> +			/*
> +			 * Don't descend into device-backed pfnmaps,
> +			 * they might refault the PMD entry.
> +			 */
> +			if (unlikely(pmd_special(*pmd)))
> +				continue;
> +
>  			split_huge_pmd(walk->vma, pmd, addr);

Note that show_numa_map() calls walk_page_vma() directly, which skips
walk_page_test() and its VM_PFNMAP filter.  The commit message and
cover letter would be clearer if they explained this explicitly: VFIO
VMAs have VM_PFNMAP set, so walk_page_range() would skip them, but
numa_maps iterates VMAs and calls walk_page_vma() per-VMA, bypassing
that filter entirely.

For the PMD case the split path also has a race window: after
split_huge_pmd() there is no pmd_none() (or pmd_leaf()) guard before
walk_pte_range().  If a concurrent thread reinstalls a huge PMD in that
window, pte_offset_map_lock() is called on what is now a leaf entry.
This is a smaller window than the PUD case (no intervening check), and
pte_offset_map_lock() may return NULL and set ACTION_AGAIN safely, but
it would be good to confirm this is handled correctly on all relevant
architectures.

> +			/*
> +			 * Don't descend into device-backed pfnmaps,
> +			 * they might refault the PUD entry.
> +			 */
> +			if (unlikely(pud_special(*pud)))
> +				continue;
> +
>  			split_huge_pud(walk->vma, pud, addr);

This handles the common reported case well.  However the race window
after split_huge_pud() is not fully closed.  If pud_special() returns
false at the check (entry is a non-special huge PUD), split_huge_pud()
is called, clears the entry, and a concurrent fault could reinstall
it as a leaf before the check below:

>  		if (pud_none(*pud))
>  			goto again;
>  
>  		err = walk_pmd_range(pud, addr, next, walk);

If the entry is reinstalled as a huge leaf rather than gone entirely,
pud_none() is false and walk_pmd_range() descends into what pmd_offset()
will misinterpret as a PMD table.  Should this be:

		if (pud_none(*pud) || pud_leaf(*pud))
			goto again;

The pud_special() guard prevents this for VFIO entries when they are
already installed at check time, which covers the reported scenario.
But VFIO's fault handler could also lose the race and install the PUD
after pud_special() reads false (entry was being concurrently installed
and atomically invisible at check time), in which case the split clears
it and VFIO faults it back in before pud_none() runs.  Adding pud_leaf()
to the guard would close that gap too.

Is there a reason to keep just the pud_none() check?

series.title: kasan: x86: arm64: KASAN tag-based mode for x86
series.author: Maciej Wieczor-Retman <m.wieczorretman@pm.me>

patches.title: [PATCH v11 00/15] kasan: x86: arm64: KASAN tag-based mode for x86
[PATCH v11 01/15] kasan: sw_tags: Use arithmetic shift for shadow computation
[PATCH v11 02/15] kasan: arm64: x86: Make special tags arch specific
[PATCH v11 04/15] x86/kasan: Add arch specific kasan functions
[PATCH v11 06/15] kasan: arm64: x86: Make page_to_virt() KASAN aware
[PATCH v11 07/15] mm/execmem: Untag addresses in EXECMEM_ROX related pointer arithmetic

review.summary: This patchset adds KASAN software tag-based mode for x86 using LAM, introducing 4-bit tags in pointer bits [60:57], arithmetic-shift shadow computation, and arch-specific tag value overrides. The approach is sound, but the new generic page_to_virt() macro in patch 06 has two correctness concerns: argument double-evaluation and silent const-dropping for const struct page * callers.
review.review_inline:
Maciej Wieczor-Retman <m.wieczorretman@pm.me>

kasan: x86: arm64: KASAN tag-based mode for x86 (patches 01, 02, 04, 06, 07)

Patches 01, 02, 04, and 07 look correct to me.  A few observations
on patch 06 below.

--- [PATCH v11 06/15] kasan: arm64: x86: Make page_to_virt() KASAN aware ---

> diff --git a/include/linux/mm.h b/include/linux/mm.h
> --- a/include/linux/mm.h
> +++ b/include/linux/mm.h
> @@ -117,7 +117,10 @@ extern int mmap_rnd_compat_bits __read_mostly;
>  #ifndef page_to_virt
> -#define page_to_virt(x)	__va(PFN_PHYS(page_to_pfn(x)))
> +#define page_to_virt(x) ({							\
> +	void *__addr = __va(PFN_PHYS(page_to_pfn((struct page *)x)));	\
> +	kasan_set_tag(__addr, page_kasan_tag(x));			\
> +})

Two concerns here.

First, x is evaluated twice: once for page_to_pfn() and again for
page_kasan_tag().  The arm64-specific page_to_virt() macros (both the
DEBUG_VIRTUAL and the fast path) correctly guard against this by
capturing the argument in a typed local variable first:

    __typeof__(x) __page = x;

If a caller passes an expression with side effects the tagged pointer
returned will be based on a different page than the address.  Adding
the same __typeof__(x) __page = x; pattern here, and using __page in
both sub-expressions, would fix this.

Second, the cast (struct page *)x silently discards the const
qualifier for callers that pass a const struct page *.  The direct
caller lowmem_page_address() is declared as:

    static __always_inline void *lowmem_page_address(const struct page *page)

and it passes page straight to page_to_virt().  The cast to
(struct page *) may trigger -Wcast-qual diagnostics on some
configurations.  The arm64 DEBUG_VIRTUAL version avoids this by using
__typeof__(x), which preserves const.  Could you do the same here
(and drop the explicit cast)?

> diff --git a/arch/arm64/include/asm/memory.h b/arch/arm64/include/asm/memory.h
> --- a/arch/arm64/include/asm/memory.h
> +++ b/arch/arm64/include/asm/memory.h
> @@ -411,11 +411,6 @@ static inline unsigned long virt_to_pfn(const void *kaddr)
>  #if defined(CONFIG_DEBUG_VIRTUAL)
> -#define page_to_virt(x)	({							\
> -	__typeof__(x) __page = x;						\
> -	void *__addr = __va(page_to_phys(__page));			\
> -	(void *)__tag_set((const void *)__addr, page_kasan_tag(__page));\
> -})

The removed arm64 DEBUG_VIRTUAL page_to_virt() called
__va(page_to_phys()), and in DEBUG_VIRTUAL builds page_to_phys()
expands to a version that includes WARN_ON_ONCE(!pfn_valid()) (see
include/asm-generic/memory_model.h).  The new generic fallback uses
PFN_PHYS(page_to_pfn()) directly, which skips that pfn_valid() check.
For an arm64 DEBUG_VIRTUAL + KASAN_SW_TAGS build, page_to_virt() will
silently lose the pfn validity warning it previously emitted.  Is
that intentional?  If not, the generic macro could use page_to_phys()
instead of the open-coded PFN_PHYS(page_to_pfn()) to preserve the
check.

series.title: mm/userfaultfd: fix hugetlb fault mutex hash calculation
series.author: Jianhui Zhou <jianhuizzzzz@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v4] mm/userfaultfd: fix hugetlb fault mutex hash calculation
[PATCH v3] mm/userfaultfd: fix hugetlb fault mutex hash calculation

review.summary: Fixes a mutex hash mismatch in mfill_atomic_hugetlb() where linear_page_index() was used to compute the page index for hugetlb_fault_mutex_hash(), but returns PAGE_SIZE units instead of the huge-page units the hash function expects. The v4 fix introduces hugetlb_linear_page_index() wrapping hstate_vma() internally, matching the formula already used by vma_hugecache_offset() in the normal fault path.

series.title: kthread, exit: clean up kernel thread exit paths
series.author: Christian Brauner <brauner@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 0/2] kthread, exit: clean up kernel thread exit paths
[PATCH v2 1/2] kthread: remove kthread_exit()
[PATCH v2 2/2] tree-wide: rename do_exit() to task_exit()

review.summary: Two-patch series: patch 1 removes kthread_exit() (which was just a macro alias for do_exit()) and replaces all uses with do_exit() directly; patch 2 renames do_exit() to task_exit() tree-wide to signal it is a public API.
review.review_inline:
Christian Brauner <brauner@kernel.org>

kthread, exit: clean up kernel thread exit paths

Patch 1 is a clean mechanical removal -- kthread_exit() was already
defined as:

    #define kthread_exit(result) do_exit(result)

so replacing all call sites with do_exit() is a runtime no-op.  The
removal of NORETURN(kthread_exit) from tools/objtool/noreturns.h is
correct.

> diff --git a/include/linux/kthread.h b/include/linux/kthread.h
> -#define kthread_exit(result) do_exit(result)

[ ... ]

> diff --git a/tools/objtool/noreturns.h b/tools/objtool/noreturns.h
> -NORETURN(kthread_exit)

These two removals are consistent.  Good.

Patch 2 renames do_exit() to task_exit().  The in-tree rename looks
mechanically complete -- I checked the base tree for remaining C/header
file references to the kernel do_exit() and found only local symbols
coincidentally named do_exit (e.g., xpc_do_exit(), do_exit_flush_lazy_tlb(),
a local do_exit() in scripts/kconfig/nconf.c).  No missed kernel call
sites.

One thing warrants discussion:

> diff --git a/kernel/exit.c b/kernel/exit.c
> -void __noreturn do_exit(long code)
> +void __noreturn task_exit(long code)

[ ... ]

> -EXPORT_SYMBOL(do_exit);
> +EXPORT_SYMBOL(task_exit);

do_exit() has been an exported kernel symbol for a very long time.
Renaming EXPORT_SYMBOL(do_exit) to EXPORT_SYMBOL(task_exit) removes
the symbol from the kernel's exported symbol table entirely.  Any
out-of-tree module that calls do_exit() directly -- without going
through module_put_and_kthread_exit() or kthread_complete_and_exit() --
will now fail to load with "Unknown symbol: do_exit".

I understand that out-of-tree modules do not have ABI guarantees, but
do_exit() is unusual in that it has been an explicitly exported public
entrypoint since very early kernel history, and some real drivers and
kthread wrappers use it directly.  Is the intent to provide a
transitional compat alias, e.g.:

    static __always_inline __noreturn void do_exit(long code)
    {
        task_exit(code);
    }

or is breaking those callers accepted as part of this cleanup?

The subsystem-visible callers in Documentation and selftests (kprobes.rst,
bpf/progs/tracing_failure.c, ftrace test scripts) that reference do_exit
by name as a probe target are all updated, which is good -- those
probe-by-symbol-name users would otherwise silently stop attaching after
the rename.

series.title: fix kmem over-charging for embedded obj_exts array
series.author: ranxiaokai627@163.com

patches.title: [PATCH 0/2] fix kmem over-charging for embedded obj_exts array
[PATCH 1/2] mm/slab: move obj_exts_in_slab() definition to slab.h
[PATCH 2/2] memcg: fix kmem over-charging for embedded obj_exts array

review.summary: The series avoids over-charging kmem for slabs whose obj_exts array lives in slab leftover space or is embedded in the object, by teaching obj_full_size() to check obj_exts_in_slab() and omit the sizeof(struct obj_cgroup *) add-on in those cases. The refactor of obj_exts_in_slab() into slab.h is clean, but patch 2 is based on an older mm-new revision and the alloc-hook hunk will not apply to the current tree.
review.review_inline:
Ran Xiaokai <ran.xiaokai@zte.com.cn>

mm/slab: move obj_exts_in_slab() definition to slab.h
memcg: fix kmem over-charging for embedded obj_exts array

Patch 1 is a clean refactor; the moved helper is identical to the
original and the !CONFIG_SLAB_OBJ_EXT stub returns false as before.
No issues found there.

Patch 2 has a rebase problem and a resulting charge/uncharge
asymmetry risk that needs to be addressed.

> diff --git a/mm/memcontrol.c b/mm/memcontrol.c
> --- a/mm/memcontrol.c
> +++ b/mm/memcontrol.c
> @@ -3270,7 +3279,7 @@ bool __memcg_slab_post_alloc_hook
>  		 * TODO: we could batch this until slab_pgdat(slab) changes
>  		 * between iterations, with a more complicated undo
>  		 */
> -		if (obj_cgroup_charge_account(objcg, flags, obj_full_size(s),
> +		if (obj_cgroup_charge_account(objcg, flags, obj_full_size(s, slab),
>  					slab_pgdat(slab), cache_vmstat_idx(s)))
>  				return false;

obj_cgroup_charge_account() does not exist in the current mm-new tree.
It was replaced with the __consume_obj_stock / __obj_cgroup_charge /
__refill_obj_stock / __account_obj_stock sequence.  With that
refactoring, obj_full_size(s) was also lifted out of the per-object
loop into a single pre-computation at the top of the function:

    size_t obj_size = obj_full_size(s);

at a point where 'slab' is not yet known.  The alloc-hook hunk will
fail to apply as-is.

More importantly: if the patch is naively rebased by only changing the
free-hook and leaving the alloc-hook pre-computation in place, the
two sides of the accounting will diverge:

  alloc: obj_size = s->size + sizeof(struct obj_cgroup *)  (old, fixed)
  free:  obj_size = obj_full_size(s, slab)                (new, per-slab)

For a slab whose obj_exts live in leftover space, the free path would
uncharge only s->size while the alloc path charged s->size +
sizeof(struct obj_cgroup *).  The difference would never be refunded,
so the over-charging bug being fixed here would turn into a
persistent under-uncharge on free.

When rebasing, obj_full_size(s, slab) must be computed inside the
per-object loop, after slab = virt_to_slab(p[i]) and after the
alloc_slab_obj_exts() call that may place obj_exts in slab space.
Only then does obj_exts_in_slab(s, slab) reflect the final placement
for that iteration's slab.

> @@ -3289,7 +3298,7 @@ void __memcg_slab_free_hook(struct kmem_cache *s, struct slab *slab,
>  			    void **p, int objects, unsigned long obj_exts)
>  {
> -	size_t obj_size = obj_full_size(s);
> +	size_t obj_size = obj_full_size(s, slab);

The free-hook change itself is correct.  All objects in a single
__memcg_slab_free_hook() call come from the same slab, so computing
obj_full_size(s, slab) once before the loop gives the right per-object
charge for every object in the batch.  slab_obj_exts() strips
OBJEXTS_FLAGS_MASK before returning, so the pointer fed to the range
check in obj_exts_in_slab() is clean.

One thing worth confirming: for the SLAB_OBJ_EXT_IN_OBJ case,
alloc_slab_obj_exts_early() sets slab->obj_exts to point into the
first object's intra-object extension area and sets the stride to
s->size.  That address still falls within [slab_address(slab),
slab_address(slab) + slab_size(slab)), so obj_exts_in_slab() returns
true for embedded obj_exts as well.  Intentional?

series.title: binfmt_misc: restore write access before closing files opened by open_exec()
series.author: Robert Garcia <rob_garcia@163.com>

patches.title: [PATCH 6.1.y] binfmt_misc: restore write access before closing files opened by open_exec()

review.summary: Fixes a write-permission leak in bm_register_write() where open_exec() denies write access on the interpreter file but the error path closed it with filp_close() without first restoring write access via allow_write_access().
review.review_inline:
From: Robert Garcia <rob_garcia@163.com>

binfmt_misc: restore write access before closing files opened by open_exec()

The fix looks correct for the 6.1.y context.

> diff --git a/fs/binfmt_misc.c b/fs/binfmt_misc.c
> --- a/fs/binfmt_misc.c
> +++ b/fs/binfmt_misc.c

[ ... ]

> +			allow_write_access(f);
>  			filp_close(f, NULL);

The choice of allow_write_access() here is correct for 6.1.y.
do_open_execat() in that branch calls deny_write_access() directly,
so allow_write_access() is the right pair.  The exe_file_allow_write_access()
wrapper that the upstream fix uses was introduced by a later commit
(0357ef03c94e) and does not exist in 6.1.y, so the adaptation in
this backport is appropriate.

One pre-existing gap worth noting separately: put_binfmt_handler()
also calls filp_close(e->interp_file, NULL) on the successfully
registered node without a preceding allow_write_access() call.  That
means every binfmt_misc entry with MISC_FMT_OPEN_FILE set leaves
i_writecount permanently at -1 on the interpreter inode after the
entry is removed, preventing writes until the inode is evicted.  This
class of bug is not introduced by the current patch, but it is in the
same function family and should probably be addressed in a companion
fix.

series.title: cgroup: improve cgroup_file_notify() scalability
series.author: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 0/3] cgroup: improve cgroup_file_notify() scalability
[PATCH v2 1/3] cgroup: reduce cgroup_file_kn_lock hold time in cgroup_file_notify()
[PATCH v2 2/3] cgroup: add lockless fast-path checks to cgroup_file_notify()
[PATCH v2 3/3] cgroup: replace global cgroup_file_kn_lock with per-cgroup_file lock

review.summary: This series reduces cgroup_file_notify() lock contention by moving kernfs_notify() outside the critical section (patch 1), adding lockless fast-path checks before acquiring the lock (patch 2), and replacing the global spinlock with a per-cgroup_file spinlock (patch 3). The first and third patches look correct, but the second patch introduces a race between the new lockless timer_reduce() path and cgroup_rm_file()'s timer_delete_sync(), breaking a guarantee that the original code relied upon.
review.review_inline:
Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

cgroup: improve cgroup_file_notify() scalability

Patches 1 and 3 look correct.  Patch 2 has a liveness concern described
below.

--- [PATCH v2 1/3] cgroup: reduce cgroup_file_kn_lock hold time ---

The kernfs_get()/kernfs_put() bracketing around the kernfs_notify()
call looks correct and follows the existing pattern in
cgroup_file_show().

--- [PATCH v2 2/3] cgroup: add lockless fast-path checks ---

> diff --git a/kernel/cgroup/cgroup.c b/kernel/cgroup/cgroup.c
> --- a/kernel/cgroup/cgroup.c
> +++ b/kernel/cgroup/cgroup.c

[ ... ]

>  void cgroup_file_notify(struct cgroup_file *cfile)
>  {
> -	unsigned long flags;
> +	unsigned long flags, last, next;
>  	struct kernfs_node *kn = NULL;
> +
> +	if (!READ_ONCE(cfile->kn))
> +		return;
> +
> +	last = READ_ONCE(cfile->notified_at);
> +	next = last + CGROUP_FILE_NOTIFY_MIN_INTV;
> +	if (time_in_range(jiffies, last, next)) {
> +		timer_reduce(&cfile->notify_timer, next);
> +		if (timer_pending(&cfile->notify_timer))
> +			return;
> +	}

The timer_reduce() call here is lockless and based on a READ_ONCE(kn)
that may be stale.  This breaks the guarantee that cgroup_rm_file()
establishes.

In cgroup_rm_file() the sequence is:

    spin_lock_irq(&cfile->lock);
    WRITE_ONCE(cfile->kn, NULL);
    spin_unlock_irq(&cfile->lock);
    timer_delete_sync(&cfile->notify_timer);

In the original code, timer_reduce() was always called while holding
cgroup_file_kn_lock.  Because cgroup_rm_file() cleared kn under that
same lock before calling timer_delete_sync(), no new timer_reduce()
could fire after timer_delete_sync() returned.  That made the
old guarantee sound: once timer_delete_sync() returns, no timer
callback for this cfile will ever fire again.

With this patch, a concurrent cgroup_file_notify() can read kn as
non-NULL (before the WRITE_ONCE clears it), then call timer_reduce()
after timer_delete_sync() has already returned.  The timer gets
re-armed.  The eventual callback in cgroup_file_notify_timer() holds
no reference to the css that owns cfile; it is just:

    cgroup_file_notify(container_of(timer, struct cgroup_file,
                                    notify_timer));

If the css is freed (via ss->css_free() in css_free_rwork_fn()) before
this re-armed timer fires, the callback accesses freed memory.

I traced the kill_css() -> css_clear_dir() -> cgroup_rm_file() ->
timer_delete_sync() path, then percpu_ref_kill_and_confirm() ->
css_killed_ref_fn() -> queue_rcu_work(cgroup_offline_wq) ->
css_killed_work_fn() -> css_put() -> css_release() ->
queue_rcu_work(cgroup_free_wq) -> css_free_rwork_fn() ->
ss->css_free(css).  There are two queue_rcu_work() calls between
timer_delete_sync() and ss->css_free(), so css freeing takes at least
two RCU grace periods.  With CGROUP_FILE_NOTIFY_MIN_INTV = HZ/100 =
10ms, the re-armed timer is very likely to fire and self-cancel before
the css is freed in practice.  But there is no formal synchronization
that prevents the timer from outliving the css -- the invariant that
timer_delete_sync() was meant to enforce is simply absent now.

One way to restore it: move timer_reduce() back inside the
cfile->lock critical section, where it was protected by the lock
that cgroup_rm_file() also holds when it clears kn.  Alternatively,
cgroup_rm_file() could call timer_delete_sync() a second time after the
current one, but that is racy too for the same reason.

A cleaner fix is to check kn again under the lock before deciding
whether to arm the timer, keeping timer_reduce() inside the locked
region for the teardown-safety it provides.

--- [PATCH v2 3/3] cgroup: replace global lock with per-cgroup_file lock ---

The per-cfile lock approach looks correct.  The initialization order
in cgroup_add_file():

>  		timer_setup(&cfile->notify_timer, cgroup_file_notify_timer, 0);
> +		spin_lock_init(&cfile->lock);
> +		cfile->kn = kn;

Is safe because cgroup_add_file() is called under cgroup_mutex, which
excludes concurrent cgroup_rm_file().  Any concurrent
cgroup_file_notify() caller that reads kn before the WRITE_ONCE will
see NULL (cfile is zero-initialized) and bail, and the lock is
initialized before kn is exposed as non-NULL.

One observation on cgroup_file_show(): it calls kernfs_get(kn) before
the NULL check on kn.  kernfs_get() handles NULL safely (it checks
'if (kn)' internally), so this is not a bug, but the ordering is a
bit surprising.  That said, it predates this series.

series.title: kho: make sure preservations do not span multiple NUMA nodes
series.author: Pratyush Yadav <pratyush@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH 1/2] kho: make sure preservations do not span multiple NUMA nodes
[PATCH 2/2] kho: drop restriction on maximum page order

review.summary: Two-patch series: patch 1 splits cross-NUMA-node preservations in kho_preserve_pages() using a new order-reduction loop; patch 2 removes the MAX_PAGE_ORDER upper bound from kho_restore_page() to support gigantic hugepage preservation.
review.review_inline:
Author: Pratyush Yadav <pratyush@kernel.org>

kho: make sure preservations do not span multiple NUMA nodes
kho: drop restriction on maximum page order

> diff --git a/kernel/liveupdate/kexec_handover.c b/kernel/liveupdate/kexec_handover.c
> --- a/kernel/liveupdate/kexec_handover.c
> +++ b/kernel/liveupdate/kexec_handover.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -869,9 +869,17 @@ int kho_preserve_pages(struct page *page, unsigned long nr_pages)
>  	}
>  
>  	while (pfn < end_pfn) {
> -		const unsigned int order =
> +		unsigned int order =
>  			min(count_trailing_zeros(pfn), ilog2(end_pfn - pfn));
>  
> +		/*
> +		 * Make sure all the pages in a single preservation are in the
> +		 * same NUMA node. The restore machinery can not cope with a
> +		 * preservation spanning multiple NUMA nodes.
> +		 */
> +		while (pfn_to_nid(pfn) != pfn_to_nid(pfn + (1UL << order) - 1))
> +			order--;

The loop termination looks correct: at order=0, pfn_to_nid(pfn) is
compared against pfn_to_nid(pfn + 0), which is always equal, so the
loop always terminates.

However, there is a mismatch with __kho_unpreserve().  Both
kho_unpreserve_pages() and the error-cleanup path inside
kho_preserve_pages() itself call __kho_unpreserve(), which computes
orders using the plain formula:

    order = min(count_trailing_zeros(pfn), ilog2(end_pfn - pfn));

without applying the NUMA adjustment.  When the range crosses a NUMA
boundary, kho_preserve_pages() stores entries at the reduced order
(e.g. order 1 for two single-node chunks), but __kho_unpreserve()
attempts to delete a single entry at the larger order (e.g. order 2).
kho_radix_del_page() fires WARN_ON(!node->table[idx]) and returns
early, leaving stale entries in the radix tree.

This affects two callers:

1. kho_unpreserve_pages() -- the normal unpreserve path.  If a caller
   preserves a cross-node range and later unpreserves it, the radix
   tree entries are never removed.

2. The error path inside kho_preserve_pages() itself:

>  		err = __kho_preserve_order(track, pfn, order);
>  		if (err) {
>  			failed_pfn = pfn;
>  			break;
>  		}
> [ ... ]
>  	if (err)
>  		__kho_unpreserve(tree, start_pfn, failed_pfn);

   If kho_radix_add_page() fails after some NUMA-split entries have
   already been stored, __kho_unpreserve() will try to remove them
   using mismatched orders and silently miss them, resulting in leaked
   preservation entries.

The same NUMA adjustment applied in kho_preserve_pages() needs to be
present (or shared via a helper) in __kho_unpreserve().

[ ... ]

--- [PATCH 2/2] kho: drop restriction on maximum page order ---

> -	if (WARN_ON_ONCE(info.magic != KHO_PAGE_MAGIC || info.order > MAX_PAGE_ORDER))
> +	if (WARN_ON_ONCE(info.magic != KHO_PAGE_MAGIC))
>  		return NULL;
>  	nr_pages = (1 << info.order);

Removing the MAX_PAGE_ORDER bound means info.order can now exceed 30
(the point at which int arithmetic overflows on most 64-bit builds
where int is 32 bits).  The expression (1 << info.order) is computed
with int arithmetic: for info.order >= 31 this is undefined behavior.
The old guard made this safe implicitly; without it the shift needs to
be (1UL << info.order).

The same issue exists in kho_init_folio():

    unsigned long nr_pages = (1 << order);

That function is reachable from kho_restore_folio() -> kho_restore_page()
with the new unconstrained info.order, so both sites need the UL fix.

series.title: mm/slab: fix an incorrect check in obj_exts_alloc_size()

series.title: [BUG] SPARSEMEM broken on RISC-V; was:  arch, mm: consolidate initialization of SPARSE memory model
series.author: Thomas =?utf-8?Q?Wei=C3=9Fschuh?= <thomas.weissschuh@linutronix.de>

patches.title: [BUG] SPARSEMEM broken on RISC-V; was: [PATCH] arch, mm: consolidate initialization of SPARSE memory model

review.summary: Fixes a RISC-V SPARSEMEM boot crash in patch_map() by replacing the phys_to_page(__pa_symbol(addr)) round-trip for kernel text addresses with a direct __pa_symbol() call, eliminating the dependency on SPARSEMEM being initialized before apply_boot_alternatives() runs. review.summary: The series extends DAMOS quota goal-based tuning to support multiple algorithms, adding a new 'temporal' tuner that uses maximum quota until the goal is achieved then sets quota to zero. The new damos_quota_is_set() helper replaces direct esz checks to decouple quota activity detection from zero effective quota.
review.review_inline:
SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon: support multiple goal-based quota tuning algorithms

> diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c
> index c16a390152790..d351ef3bcf4e9 100644
> --- a/mm/damon/core.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/core.c

[ ... ]

> +static bool damos_quota_is_set(struct damos_quota *quota)
> +{
> +	return quota->esz || quota->sz || quota->ms ||
> +		!damos_quota_goals_empty(quota);
> +}

The commit description says this function is "decoupled from zero
effective quota," but quota->esz is still included in the OR. If the
intent is to check only user-set constraints (sz, ms, goals), what
purpose does the quota->esz term serve? In practice it appears
redundant: if esz is nonzero, at least one of sz/ms/goals must have
been set (since damos_set_effective_quota early-returns with esz=0
when all three are unset). But including it contradicts the stated
coupling.

[ ... ]

> +		if (damos_quota_is_set(quota) &&
> +				quota->charged_sz >= quota->esz)
>  			s->stat.qt_exceeds++;

With the temporal tuner achieving its goal, damos_set_effective_quota
sets quota->esz to 0.  At the start of the next charge window,
charged_sz is reset to 0.  Then this check evaluates:
damos_quota_is_set() = true (goals list is non-empty), and
0 >= 0 = true, so qt_exceeds is incremented every window while the
scheme is dormant.

The old check, quota->esz && quota->charged_sz >= quota->esz, would
not count this case because esz=0 makes the first term false.

The result is that qt_exceeds is inflated every charge window when the
temporal tuner has achieved its goal and is keeping the scheme
deactivated. This makes the stat misleading for temporal-mode users
watching it to understand how often the scheme hit its quota.

Should the qt_exceeds increment be guarded by esz > 0, or limited to
cases where the scheme was actually applying work (charged_sz > 0)?

[ ... ]

> +static void damos_goal_tune_esz_bp_temporal(struct damos_quota *quota)
> +{
> +	unsigned long score = damos_quota_score(quota);
> +
> +	if (score >= 10000)
> +		quota->esz_bp = 0;
> +	else if (quota->sz)
> +		quota->esz_bp = quota->sz * 10000;
> +	else
> +		quota->esz_bp = ULONG_MAX;
> +}

quota->sz * 10000 overflows unsigned long if quota->sz exceeds
ULONG_MAX / 10000 (roughly 1.8 PB on 64-bit). This is far beyond any
practical memory size today, but the inconsistency with the ULONG_MAX
path in the else branch is worth noting. Should this use a saturating
multiply or a min(quota->sz, ULONG_MAX / 10000) * 10000 form to
match the intent of the else branch?

series.title: mm: Do not allocate shrinker info with cgroup.memory=nokmem
series.author: =?utf-8?q?Michal_Koutn=C3=BD?= <mkoutny@suse.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v2] mm: Do not allocate shrinker info with cgroup.memory=nokmem

review.summary: When cgroup.memory=nokmem is configured, skip registering slab shrinkers in the per-memcg shrinker IDR so they fall back to a global nr_deferred counter, while preserving full memcg-aware behavior for SHRINKER_NONSLAB shrinkers (e.g. deferred_split_shrinker) that reclaim non-kernel memory.
review.review_inline:
From: Michal Koutny <mkoutny@suse.com>
Subject: [PATCH v2] mm: Do not allocate shrinker info with cgroup.memory=nokmem

> diff --git a/mm/shrinker.c b/mm/shrinker.c
> --- a/mm/shrinker.c
> +++ b/mm/shrinker.c

[ ... ]

> +	if (mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled() && !(shrinker->flags & SHRINKER_NONSLAB))
> +		return -ENOSYS;

The logic here is correct.  The -ENOSYS return causes shrinker_alloc()
to clear SHRINKER_MEMCG_AWARE and fall to the non_memcg label, so
slab shrinkers get a plain global nr_deferred.  NONSLAB shrinkers
(|(shrinker->flags & SHRINKER_NONSLAB)| is true) skip this check,
keep their IDR entry and SHRINKER_MEMCG_AWARE, and continue to
participate in shrink_slab_memcg() per-memcg walks.

Using mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled() (the boot-time cgroup_memory_nokmem
constant) instead of memcg_kmem_online() (the runtime static key) is
the right choice: memcg_kmem_online() is false until the first
non-root memcg is created, so any shrinker registered during boot
would be incorrectly stripped of SHRINKER_MEMCG_AWARE even on a
normal (non-nokmem) system.

> @@ -716,6 +718,7 @@ struct shrinker *shrinker_alloc(unsigned int flags, const char *fmt, ...)
>  	 *  - non-memcg-aware shrinkers
>  	 *  - !CONFIG_MEMCG
>  	 *  - memcg is disabled by kernel command line
> +	 *  - non-slab shrinkers: when memcg kmem is disabled
>  	 */

The new bullet appears to describe the wrong class of shrinker.  The
non_memcg label is reached by shrinkers that need a global nr_deferred.
With nokmem configured:

  - Slab shrinkers (without SHRINKER_NONSLAB) get -ENOSYS from
    shrinker_memcg_alloc(), have SHRINKER_MEMCG_AWARE cleared, and
    fall through to non_memcg -- they DO allocate nr_deferred here.

  - NONSLAB shrinkers bypass the new check, shrinker_memcg_alloc()
    succeeds, shrinker_alloc() returns before reaching non_memcg --
    they do NOT allocate nr_deferred here.

So the bullet that describes what lands at non_memcg should read
"slab shrinkers" (or "shrinkers without SHRINKER_NONSLAB"), not
"non-slab shrinkers".

series.title: mm: batch TLB flushing for dirty folios in vmscan
series.author: Zhang Peng via B4 Relay <devnull+zippermonkey.icloud.com@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH 0/2] mm: batch TLB flushing for dirty folios in vmscan
[PATCH 1/2] mm/vmscan: refactor shrink_folio_list for readability and maintainability
[PATCH 2/2] mm, vmscan: flush TLB for every 31 folios evictions

review.summary: This series optimizes vmscan by batching dirty anon/shmem folios and flushing TLB once per batch instead of per-folio. Patch 1 refactors shrink_folio_list into helper functions and changes the return type from unsigned int to void, tracking reclaimed pages via stat->nr_reclaimed. Patch 2 accumulates dirty folios in a folio_batch and flushes TLB for the whole batch before calling pageout.
review.review_inline:
Zhang Peng via B4 Relay <devnull+zippermonkey.icloud.com@kernel.org>

mm: batch TLB flushing for dirty folios in vmscan

The overall approach is sound and the performance numbers are impressive.
A few correctness and design concerns below.

> +static void pageout_batch(struct folio_batch *fbatch,
> +			  struct list_head *ret_folios,
> +			  struct folio_batch *free_folios,
> +			  struct scan_control *sc, struct reclaim_stat *stat,
> +			  struct swap_iocb **plug, struct list_head *folio_list)
> +{
> +	int i = 0, count = folio_batch_count(fbatch);
> +	struct folio *folio;
> +
> +	folio_batch_reinit(fbatch);
> +	do {
> +		folio = fbatch->folios[i];
> +		if (!folio_trylock(folio)) {
> +			list_add(&folio->lru, ret_folios);
> +			continue;
> +		}
> +
> +		if (folio_test_writeback(folio) || folio_test_lru(folio) ||
> +		    folio_mapped(folio))
> +			goto next;
> +		folio_batch_add(fbatch, folio);
> +		continue;
> +	next:
> +		folio_unlock(folio);
> +		list_add(&folio->lru, ret_folios);
> +	} while (++i != count);

The first loop calls folio_batch_reinit(fbatch) (setting fbatch->nr=0)
then reads from fbatch->folios[i] and compacts passing folios back into
fbatch via folio_batch_add(fbatch, folio).  This relies on two
implementation details of folio_batch: (a) reinit does not zero the
folios[] array, and (b) folio_batch_add writes to folios[nr] and since
nr <= i at all times there is no aliasing.  Both hold today, but neither
is documented.  Please add a comment explaining this invariant so future
changers of folio_batch do not silently break this loop.

> +	/* One TLB flush for the batch */
> +	try_to_unmap_flush_dirty();
> +	do {
> +		folio = fbatch->folios[i];
> +		pageout_one(folio, ret_folios, free_folios, sc, stat, plug,
> +			    folio_list);
> +	} while (++i != count);
> +	folio_batch_reinit(fbatch);

All folios that passed the first-loop filter are held locked
simultaneously here, across every pageout_one() call in the second loop.
For async I/O (the common case), writepage() releases the folio lock
before returning, so each folio's lock window is short.  But for the
synchronous path (shmem backed by slow NFS, or a ramdisk hit that takes
longer than expected), pageout_one() can stall with all up to 31 locks
held.  Any thread wanting to fault in folio[k] will block until all
folios[0..k-1] have been paged out.  The commit message mentions this
concern briefly; it would be good to spell out what workloads are at
risk and why they are considered acceptable.

[ ... ]

> +			/*
> +			 * For anon, we should only see swap cache (anon) and
> +			 * the list pinning the page. For file page, the filemap
> +			 * and the list pins it. Combined with the page_ref_freeze
> +			 * in pageout_batch ensure nothing else touches the page
> +			 * during lock unlocked.
> +			 */
> +			folio_unlock(folio);
> +			if (!folio_batch_add(&flush_folios, folio))
> +				pageout_batch(&flush_folios,
> +							&ret_folios, &free_folios,
> +							sc, stat, &plug,
> +							folio_list);
> +			goto next;

The comment says "page_ref_freeze in pageout_batch", but pageout_batch
has no page_ref_freeze.  The freeze happens later inside
__remove_mapping(), which is only reached for folios that pass pageout
all the way to folio_free().  During the window between folio_unlock()
here and the relock in pageout_batch, the folio's refcount is not frozen
and arbitrary speculative references can be taken.  For swap-backed shmem
folios the folio is still in the shmem page cache, so a concurrent fault
can re-map it.  folio_mapped() in pageout_batch detects and handles this
correctly, but the comment is misleading and should be fixed.

[ ... ]

> +	if (folio_batch_count(&flush_folios)) {
> +		pageout_batch(&flush_folios, &ret_folios, &free_folios, sc,
> +			      stat, &plug, folio_list);
> +	}
>  	/* 'folio_list' is always empty here */

The end-of-loop drain calls pageout_batch(), which calls pageout_one(),
which calls pageout(), which calls shmem_writeout().  shmem_writeout()
can split a large shmem folio and add the resulting tail folios back to
folio_list (see writeout() comment: "the split out folios get added back
to folio_list").  In the inline-batch case (when folio_batch_add returns
0 mid-loop) these tail folios are picked up immediately by the main while
loop; that is fine.  But in the end-of-loop drain the main while loop has
already exited, so the tail folios sit in folio_list until the caller's
move_folios_to_lru() puts them back on the LRU.  They will be reclaimed
on a future pass.  Two consequences:

1. The comment "'folio_list' is always empty here" is now potentially
   false.  It should be updated.

2. This is a latent behavior change for large shmem folios: in the
   original code, tail pages from a split were always reclaimed in the
   same shrink_folio_list() call; with this patch they may need an extra
   pass.  The series claims no functional change in patch 1, but patch 2
   introduces this difference.

Is this considered acceptable?  If so, the comment needs to be fixed and
ideally a note added explaining why the tail-folio handling changed.

[ ... ]

series.title: mm/swap: enhance swap cluster allocation checks
series.author: Hui Zhu <hui.zhu@linux.dev>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 0/2] mm/swap: enhance swap cluster allocation checks
[PATCH v2 1/2] mm/swap: Add VM_WARN_ON to isolate_lock_cluster()
[PATCH v2 2/2] mm/swap: Add lockdep for si->global_cluster_lock in swap_cluster_alloc_table()

review.summary: The series adds a VM_WARN_ON to catch full clusters reaching swap_cluster_alloc_table(), and reorders lockdep_assert_held() calls to match actual lock acquisition order while adding the missing global_cluster_lock assertion for non-SSD devices. Patch 2 is correct, but patch 1 contains a dead assertion.
review.review_inline:
Author: Hui Zhu <zhuhui@kylinos.cn>

mm/swap: Add VM_WARN_ON to isolate_lock_cluster()

> diff --git a/mm/swapfile.c b/mm/swapfile.c
> --- a/mm/swapfile.c
> +++ b/mm/swapfile.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -596,6 +596,9 @@ static struct swap_cluster_info *isolate_lock_cluster(
>  	spin_unlock(&si->lock);
>  
>  	if (found && !cluster_table_is_alloced(found)) {
> +		/* Table of full cluster must be allocated. */
> +		VM_WARN_ON(ci->flags == CLUSTER_FLAG_FULL);
> +
>  		/* Only an empty free cluster's swap table can be freed. */
>  		VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(list != &si->free_clusters);

The new assertion checks ci->flags, but that field was already cleared
to CLUSTER_FLAG_NONE inside the loop body before the break:

    flags = ci->flags;        /* saves original flags */
    ci->flags = CLUSTER_FLAG_NONE;  /* clears ci->flags */
    found = ci;
    break;

So ci->flags == CLUSTER_FLAG_FULL will never be true here, making this
a dead assertion.  The existing VM_WARN_ON_ONCE checks in the same block
already use the saved local variable 'flags' for exactly this reason:

    VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(flags != CLUSTER_FLAG_FREE);

Should the new check also use 'flags'?

    VM_WARN_ON(flags == CLUSTER_FLAG_FULL);

Also, the surrounding assertions use VM_WARN_ON_ONCE; was the choice of
VM_WARN_ON (without _ONCE) here intentional?

series.title: selftests/mm: add THP sysfs interface test
series.author: Breno Leitao <leitao@debian.org>

patches.title: [PATCH] selftests/mm: add THP sysfs interface test

review.summary: Adds a new shell-based selftest that exercises the THP sysfs interface: global enabled, defrag, use_zero_page, hpage_pmd_size, shmem_enabled, shrink_underused, khugepaged tunables, and per-size anon/shmem/stats files, with state save and restore. The boundary values tested were verified against the actual kernel sysfs store handlers.
review.review_inline:
Author: Breno Leitao <leitao@debian.org>

selftests/mm: add THP sysfs interface test

This adds a shell-based selftest for the THP sysfs interface, covering
global and per-size knobs, khugepaged tunables, and per-size stats.
Boundary checks are correct: max_ptes_{none,swap,shared} upper limit of
HPAGE_PMD_NR-1 matches KHUGEPAGED_MAX_PTES_LIMIT, and the pages_to_scan
reject-zero check matches the `if (err || !pages)` guard in
pages_to_scan_store().  State save/restore and KTAP usage look correct.

One issue with the run_vmtests.sh integration:

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/run_vmtests.sh b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/run_vmtests.sh
> +CATEGORY="thp" run_test ./thp_sysfs_test.sh

The run_test() helper, when CATEGORY is "thp" or "hugetlb", sets
skip=1 if HAVE_HUGEPAGES is not 1:

    if [ ${CATEGORY} == "thp" -o ${CATEGORY} == "hugetlb" ]; then
        if [ "${HAVE_HUGEPAGES}" = "1" ]; then
            echo 3 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches
            ...
        else
            echo "hugepages not supported" | tap_prefix
            skip=1
        fi
    fi

HAVE_HUGEPAGES=0 is set when /proc/meminfo lacks HugePages_Free or
Hugepagesize, which happens on kernels without CONFIG_HUGETLB_PAGE.
That configuration is independent of CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE.
A kernel with THP enabled but without HugeTLB page support would
have /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage present but would cause
thp_sysfs_test.sh to be silently skipped here.

The other tests in this category (khugepaged, transhuge-stress) need
actual huge page allocations, so the skip makes sense for them.  Could
thp_sysfs_test.sh be moved to a category that does not gate on
HAVE_HUGEPAGES, or could the thp_sysfs_test.sh call be placed outside
of the hugepages guard and handled with its own existence check like
some other tests do?

series.title: fix: mm: memcontrol: convert objcg to be per-memcg per-node type
series.author: Qi Zheng <qi.zheng@linux.dev>

patches.title: [PATCH] fix: mm: memcontrol: convert objcg to be per-memcg per-node type

review.summary: In the error path of mem_cgroup_css_online(), orig_objcg is dropped via obj_cgroup_put() but the pointer is not cleared to NULL, causing __mem_cgroup_free() to call obj_cgroup_put() on it a second time. This patch sets pn->orig_objcg to NULL after the put so the second call in __mem_cgroup_free() is a safe no-op.
review.review_inline:
From: Qi Zheng <zhengqi.arch@bytedance.com>

fix: mm: memcontrol: convert objcg to be per-memcg per-node type

Reset pn->orig_objcg to NULL to prevent obj_cgroup_put()
from being called agagin in __mem_cgroup_free().

> diff --git a/mm/memcontrol.c b/mm/memcontrol.c
> --- a/mm/memcontrol.c
> +++ b/mm/memcontrol.c

[ ... ]

> -		if (pn && pn->orig_objcg)
> +		if (pn && pn->orig_objcg) {
>  			obj_cgroup_put(pn->orig_objcg);
> +			/*
> +			 * Reset pn->orig_objcg to NULL to prevent obj_cgroup_put()
> +			 * from being called agagin in __mem_cgroup_free().
                                              ^^^^^^

Tiny nit: "agagin" should be "again".

The fix itself is correct.  obj_cgroup_put() in the non-CONFIG_MEMCG
stub is a no-op, and in the real implementation it guards with
"if (objcg && ...)", so __mem_cgroup_free() calling it with NULL is
harmless.  The double-put in the error path is real: for every node
that succeeded in obj_cgroup_alloc() before the failure at node k,
the error-path loop drops the extra reference taken by
obj_cgroup_get(), but without this NULL assignment __mem_cgroup_free()
will drop it a second time.

series.title: mm/damon: let DAMON be paused and resumed
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [RFC PATCH 00/10] mm/damon: let DAMON be paused and resumed
[RFC PATCH 01/10] mm/damon/core: introduce damon_ctx->paused
